title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a Set in Pharo, you can use the `includes:` message. This message returns a boolean value indicating whether the Set contains the given element or not.

Here's how you can modify your code to check if the number already exists in the Set before adding it:

```smalltalk
MyFunction | list length data z x |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data := received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do: [ :k | | list |
    list := data at: k.
    list < x ifTrue: [
        | existingElement |
        existingElement := z includes: list.
        (existingElement ifNotNil: [ ^ self ]) "If the element already exists, exit the loop and do not add the new element"
        z add: list
    ]
]
```

In the above code, after checking if the number is less than the threshold `x`, we check if the number (as an element in the form of a Set) already exists in the Set using the `includes:` message. If it does, we exit the loop without adding the number to the Set. If it doesn't, we add it to the Set as usual.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch statement in Pharo Smalltalk, you can use nested ifElseIf statements instead. In your code, you have already started with this approach but there are some improvements that can be made.

First, let's clarify the problem: The code you have provided is trying to parse a command and an integer from a string, and then execute a method based on the command. However, the code is not working as expected, and you're having trouble debugging it.

Here's the suggested solution for your code:

1. First, make sure that the input string is properly formatted, and that the 'down', 'left', 'right', and 'up' commands are always followed by an integer value.

2. Improve error handling by adding a check for a valid command before attempting to execute any commands.

3. Use nested ifElseIf statements to implement the switch-like behavior.

Here's the updated code:

```Smalltalk
| commands i bo val valInt |
commands := aTurtleProgram copy. % make a copy to avoid mutating the original string while parsing

[commands size = 0] whileFalse: [
    i := commands findIndex: [ :each | each beginsWith: ' ' ]. % find the index of the command
    i ifNil: [ ^ self error: 'Invalid command format' ].
    i < commands size ifTrue: [
        i := i + 1. % point to the command
        bo := commands at: i.
        val := commands at: i + 1, ifNil: [ ^ self error: 'Missing value for command' ].
        valInt := val asInteger.

        % Check for valid commands
        bo = 'down' ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ].
        bo = 'left' ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ].
        bo = 'right' ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ].
        bo = 'up' ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ].
        bo ifFalse: [ % handle invalid commands here, e.g. print a warning or error message
            self assert: false ] ]

    % remove parsed commands from the list
    commands removeFirst: i + 1.
].

% Handle remaining commands in the list, if any
commands notEmpty ifTrue: [ ^ self error: 'Unparsed commands found: ', commands asString ]
```

This updated code uses error handling, checks for valid commands, and uses nested ifElseIf statements to implement the switch-like behavior. If you still encounter issues, make sure that the input string is properly formatted and that the error handling messages are displayed in your debugger.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.

```smalltalk

"Define the command to be executed"
command := 'ls -l'.

"Create a new process with the defined command and capture its output"
Process new

  standardOutputDo: [ :line | commandOutput := commandOutput, line, '\' ].

"Print the captured output"
Transcript show: 'Command output:'; Transcript cr; show: commandOutput.
```

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Pharo and Squeak Smalltalk, the Date and DateAndTime classes do not provide a constructor method that directly accepts three arguments for year, month, and day. Instead, you can create a new Date or DateAndTime object using existing methods and some arithmetic.

To create a Date object, you can use the `new:` class method on Date and then set the month, day, and year using the `year:month:day:` message. Here's an example:

```Smalltalk
| newDate |
newDate := Date new: 1995.
newDate year: 1995.
newDate month: 1.
newDate day: 1.
```

In this example, we first create a new Date object using the `new:` message, and then set the year, month, and day using subsequent messages.

For a DateAndTime object, you can use the `new:` class method on DateAndTime and set the year, month, day, hour, minute, and second using messages. Here's an example:

```Smalltalk
| newDateTime |
newDateTime := DateAndTime new: 2022. January 1 at: 12:30.
newDateTime year: 2022.
newDateTime month: 1.
newDateTime day: 1.
newDateTime hour: 12.
newDateTime minute: 30.
newDateTime second: 0.
```

In this example, we first create a new DateAndTime object using the `new:` message, and then set the year, month, day, hour, minute, and second using subsequent messages.

Therefore, there isn't a predefined method that directly accepts three arguments for year, month, and day to create a new Date or DateAndTime object in one step. However, you can achieve the desired result by creating a new object and setting the values using messages.

a PluggableDictionary(

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

)

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, you can sort a `Dictionary` by values using the `collect:` message with a block that extracts the values from the key-value pairs, and then using an `OrderedCollection` and the `sort:` message to sort the resulting collection based on the extracted values in descending order.

Here's an example of how to achieve that:

First, let's assume you have a `PluggableDictionary` named `myDictionary` as defined in your question.

```smalltalk
| myDictionary |
myDictionary := #( 'Rankable1' -> 8.5
                 'Rankable2' -> 9.0
               ).
```

Now, let's sort the `myDictionary` based on the values in descending order:

```smalltalk
| sortedCollection |
sortedCollection := myDictionary collect: [ :each | each key ]
                         thenCollect: [ :keys | (myDictionary at: keys first) ]
                         thenSort: [ :val1 :val2 | val1 > val2 ].
```

The `collect:` message is used twice here. The first time it is used to create a new collection (`sortedCollection`) containing the keys of the dictionary. The second time it is used inside the first block to create a new collection (`thenCollect:`) that contains the corresponding values of those keys.

The `thenSort:` message is used to sort the resulting collection based on the extracted values in descending order.

body:

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation:
Pharo and Squeak are two popular open-source implementations of the Smalltalk programming language, both of which serve as excellent choices for a beginning Smalltalker. Pharo, which started as a fork of Squeak in 2008, is a modern, dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax and a live coding environment that allows modification of code while it runs (Pharo, p. 1). The current version of Pharo is 5.0, released in May 2016, with version 6.0 in alpha development (Pharo, p. 2). Pharo is highly portable and can run on various operating systems, including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi (Pharo, p. 2).

One significant difference between Pharo and Squeak is that Pharo only includes code contributed under the MIT license (Pharo, p. 1), whereas Squeak includes both MIT and BSD-style licenses. Pharo also emphasizes evolutionary and incremental changes, which keeps it relatively stable while experimenting (Pharo, p. 2). Additionally, Pharo has a rich library and powerful IDE, providing essential tools for managing complex code and promoting good design (Pharo, p. 1).

Pharo is the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework and is used in various innovative projects, from multimedia applications and educational platforms to commercial web development environments (Pharo, p. 1). The community welcomes coders from all corners of the world and values changes and improvements (Pharo, p. 1).

While there isn't a clear favorite between Pharo and Squeak, the choice depends on your specific needs and preferences. Pharo's focus on evolutionary changes and MIT license might appeal to some, while others may prefer Squeak's larger community and support for both MIT and BSD licenses.

To install Pharo, visit http://pharo.org/download and click the button for your operating system (Pharo, p. 7). After installation, you can launch Pharo and begin exploring its features.

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
Yes, in Pharo Smalltalk, it is possible to add a string to a String instance via methods. A String in Pharo is a collection of Characters and is mutable, meaning its content can be changed. There are several ways to modify a String, such as using the at:put: message or the comma concatenation operator.

Here's an example using the at:put: message:

```smalltalk
| s |
s := 'no hurries'.
s at: 4 put: $h; at: 5 put: $u.
s
>>> 'no hurries'
>>> 'no hurries' asString
>>> 'no hurries'
s at: 4 put: 'hi'; yourself.
s
>>> 'no hi urries'
```

You can also use the comma concatenation operator:

```smalltalk
| s |
s := 'no'.
s := s, 'worries'.
s
>>> 'no worries'
```

However, the behavior you are looking for, where you can pass a String instance to multiple methods, each adding some information to it, is more like the "html" object in Seaside. In this case, you might want to consider using a WriteStream instead. WriteStreams are useful for appending a lot of data to a String at various locations.

```smalltalk
| string stream |
string := String new.
stream := WriteStream on: string.
(1 to: 100000) do: [:i | stream nextPutAll: i asString; space ].
stream close.
string.
>>> '1 2 3 .... 100000'
```

Keep in mind that Pharo has an abstract class called String, but when we instantiate a String, we actually get either an 8-bit ByteString or a 32-bit WideString. However, we usually ignore the difference and just talk about instances of String.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, you can check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it using various methods. One common way is to use the `isMemberOf:` message, which returns true if the object is an exact instance of the given class. Another method is `isKindOf:` which returns true if the receiver's class is the same as or a subclass of the argument class.

Here's an example:

```smalltalk
| anObject aClass |
anObject := 1.
aClass := SmallInteger class.

anObject isMemberOf: aClass ifFalse: [
    Transcript show: 'An object is not an instance of the given class'; yourself.
    ^ anObject.
].
anObject isKindOf: aClass ifFalse: [
    Transcript show: 'An object is not a subclass of the given class'; yourself.
    ^ anObject.
].

Transcript show: 'An object is an instance of the given class or a subclass of it'; yourself.
```

This code checks if the number `1` is an instance of the class `SmallInteger` or a subclass of it. If it is, it prints "An object is an instance of the given class or a subclass of it". Otherwise, it prints either "An object is not an instance of the given class" or "An object is not a subclass of the given class".

You can also use `isKindOf:` to check if an object is an instance of a class or any of its subclasses. For instance, `1 isKindOf: Number` will return true because Number is a superclass of SmallInteger and Integer, which is the class of 1.

Pharo's development tools, such as the system browser, debugger, and inspector, use these reflective features to provide functionality. For more information on classes and metaclasses in Pharo, refer to pages 183-184 and 347-357 of the Pharo By Example textbook.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
The issue with printing an integer to the Transcript in Pharo using the code `Transcript show: (myNum printString)` with a SmallInteger variable `myNum` results in a crash. This is likely due to the fact that the Transcript is expecting a String message, and an integer is being sent instead.

To print an integer to the Transcript, you can simply use the `Transcript show:` message with a String argument. For example:

```smalltalk
| myNum |
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString). "This will print the integer as a string"
Transcript show: 'The number is: ', myNum printString. "This will print 'The number is: ' followed by the integer as a string"
```

You can also use the `Transcript cr` message to add a newline before the printed message, as in:

```smalltalk
Transcript cr.
Transcript show: 'The number is: '.
Transcript show: myNum printString.
```

This will print 'The number is: ' followed by the integer on separate lines in the Transcript.

It's important to note that the Transcript is an object used for logging system messages and is a kind of system console in Pharo. Playgrounds are useful for typing and experimenting with code snippets or any text you would like to remember. To inspect an object, you can use the Inspector tool instead of printing it directly to the Transcript.

Additionally, if you'd like to persist objects, you can use various systems such as Fuel, STON, or a database.

For more information, you can refer to the Pharo By Example book, specifically pages 16, 17, and 18.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
To match all words in a phrase using regex in Pharo Smalltalk, you can make use of the `matchesIn:` and `matchesIn:collect:` messages provided by the regex API. These methods return collections of matches and allow you to perform certain operations on the matched substrings.

Your current regex, `[A-Za-z]*`, will match any sequence of characters that consist of letters. However, it doesn't specifically target words. To achieve your desired result, you can use the following regex:

```smalltalk
'\<([^aeiou]|[a])+\>' asRegex
```

This regex pattern matches words with one or more consonants or the letter 'a' preceded by a non-vowel. The `\<` character matches the beginning of a word.

To match all words in a phrase 'hello how are you', you can use:

```smalltalk
'hello how are you' matchesIn: regex collect: [:each | each asString]
```

This will return a collection containing the strings 'hello', 'how', and 'are you'.

If you want to ignore case while matching words, you can use the `matchesIn:do:` or `matchesIn:collect:` messages with the `matchesRegexIgnoringCase:` or `prefixMatchesRegexIgnoringCase:` messages.

For example, to match all words in a phrase while ignoring case:

```smalltalk
'hello how are you' regex: '\<([^aeiou]|[a])+\>' matchesIn: 'Hello How Are You' collect: [:each | each asString]
```

This will also return a collection containing the strings 'hello', 'how', and 'are you'.

References:
- Pages 339, 340, and 344 from the "Pharo By Example" textbook.
- Pharo Smalltalk regex documentation.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, both `self` and `yourself` represent the receiver of a message, but they behave differently in method execution and method lookup.

The `self` keyword refers to the object that receives the message and initiates the method execution. Method lookup starts in the class of the receiver. On the other hand, `yourself` is less commonly used and it is equivalent to explicitly returning the receiver from a method. In the absence of an explicit return statement, a method returns the receiver as its result.

When you explicitly write `^ self`, it is not a good practice as it communicates that you are returning something of interest to the sender, but in this case, you're simply returning the receiver.

There is also a concept of `super` in Smalltalk, which might lead to confusion. Unlike common belief, `super` does not refer to the superclass; instead, it refers to the receiver of the message in the context of a superclass method. The difference lies in the method lookup: instead of starting in the class of the receiver, method lookup begins in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.

For instance, consider the following example:
```smalltalk
Object subclass: #WebServer
    instanceVariableNames: 'port'
    classVariableNames: ''
    poolDictionaries: ''
    category: 'Web'

WebServer class >> uniqueInstance
    ^ self new initialize.

WebServer class >> new: port
    ^ self new initialize: port.

WebServer >> initialize: port
    self port: port.

ReliableWebServer class new: 8020
```
In this example, `self` is bound to the respective receiver, either `WebServer` or `ReliableWebServer`. Using `self` ensures that the correct class is returned when creating an instance. `super` does not exist in this context, but if it did, the lookup would start in the superclass `Object` instead of `WebServer`.

Additionally, it is important to note that Pharo uses the receiver to perform method lookup and calls, so `self` is a pseudo-variable representing the receiver and is called the "receiver of a message."

Confusion may arise when comparing `self` and `yourself` to `super` and `superclass`. In summary:

- `self`: Represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver.
- `yourself`: Equivalent to explicitly returning the receiver from a method.
- `super`: Represents the receiver of the message in the context of a superclass method, and method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the `super` send occurs.

Sources:
- Pharo By Example, pages 60, 103, 105, 109.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, you don't initialize instance variables using a constructor with parameters like in languages such as Java or C++. Instead, you can initialize an instance variable directly in the class side, using the `initialize` class method or lazy initialization. This is because Pharo manages the object lifecycle differently, and users typically expect the class to be "ready" to return new instances without having to call initialization methods explicitly.

When defining a new instance variable, it's recommended to avoid initializing it in accessor methods, unless you have a specific reason for doing so. Instead, you can initialize it directly in the class side, as shown below:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: 'myVariable'
    classVariableNames: ''
    package: 'MyPackage'

MyClass class >> initialize
    Self new initializeMyVariable: 42.

MyClass new initialize
```

Here, we define a new class `MyClass` and initialize its instance variable `myVariable` to 42 using the `initializeMyVariable:` class method in the `initialize` class method. Then, when creating a new instance of `MyClass`, the `initialize` method will be called automatically, and the `myVariable` instance variable will be initialized accordingly.

Pharo provides three types of shared variables: globally shared variables, class variables, and pool variables. Global variables are stored in the `Smalltalk` instance of the `SystemDictionary` class and are accessible everywhere. Class variables are shared between instances and classes, while pool variables are shared among a group of classes.

To define a global variable, you can simply assign a value to an undeclared identifier, and the parser will offer to declare it for you. To define a global programmatically, you can use the `Smalltalk globals at:` and `put:` messages. Class variables are initialized in the `initialize` class method, using lazy initialization or initialization when an instance is created, depending on the specific use case.

Regarding your question about initializing a `Color` object with a specific color name, you can create a new `Color` instance and set its name property using the dot (.) notation:

```smalltalk
| aRedColor |
aRedColor := Color new.
aRedColor name: 'red'.
```

Alternatively, you can use the class method `Color red` to create a new `Color` instance with the 'red' name already set:

```smalltalk
| aRedColor |
aRedColor := Color red.
```

Both methods achieve the same result. The choice between them depends on your personal preference and the specific use case.

title: For loop for array in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to make an array with random numbers (just 0 or 1), but when I run it, it just prints this: End of statement list encountered ->

This is my code:

GenList
 | lista |
  lista := Array new: 31.
  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

What can I do?
stackoverflow_answer:
Some interesting things to consider:

1. The method selector doesn't start with a lowercase letter

It is a tradition for selectors to start with a lowercase letter. In this sense, genLista would be more correct than GenLista.

2. The method selector includes the abbreviated word 'gen'

For instance, genLista could be renamed to genereLista o listaAlAzar (if you decide to use Spanish)

3. The Array named lista has 31 elements, not 30

The result of Array new: 31 is an array of 31 elements. However, the code below it only fills 30 of them, leaving the last one uninitialized (i.e., nil). Possible solution: lista := Array new: 30.

4. A dot is missing causing a compilation error

The code

  1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom - 1]
  ^lista

does not compile because there is no dot indicating the separation between the two sentences. Note that the error happens at compilation time (i.e., when you save the method) because the return token ^ must start a statement (i.e., it cannot be inlined inside a statement).

There are other cases where a missing dot will not prevent the code from compiling. Instead, an error will happen at runtime. Here is a (typical) example:

1 to: 10 do: [:i | self somethingWith: i]     "<- missing dot here"
self somethingElse

the missing dot will generate the runtime error self not understood by block.

5. There is a more expressive way of generating 0s and 1s at random

The calculation 2 atRandom - 1 is ok. However, it forces the reader to mentally do the math. A better way to reveal your intention would have been

#(0 1) atRandom

6. When playing with random numbers don't forget to save the seed

While it is ok to use atRandom, such a practice should only be used with "toy" code. If you are developing a system or a library, the recommended practice is to save the seed somewhere before generating any random data. This will allow you to reproduce the generation of random quantities later on for the sake of debugging or confirmation. (Note however, that this will not suffice for making your program deterministically reproducible because unordered (e.g. hashed) collections could form differently in successive executions.)
explanation:
The issue with your code (p. 1) is that you're trying to put values into an Array starting from index 1, but you're using the message "put:" instead of "at:put:". In Pharo Smalltalk, arrays are zero-indexed, but when you create a new array using "Array new: 31" or initialize an array with a fixed size, the first element is at index 1, not 0.

To make your code work, you should modify it to use the "at:" message to access and update the elements of the array. Here's an example of how you can modify your code:

```Smalltalk
GenList
 | lista |
 lista := Array new: 31.
 1 to: 30 do: [ :i | lista at: i put: 2 atRandom].
 ^lista
```

Additionally, it's important to note that when you're using a loop to iterate over an array or a collection, you should use the message "do:" instead of "to:do:" to avoid confusion. "do:" is the basic collection iterator that applies its argument (a block) to each element of the receiver. Here's an example of how you can use "do:" to print the elements of an array:

```Smalltalk
| myArray |
myArray := #(1 2 3 4 5).
myArray do: [ :each | Transcript show: each; cr ].
```

By using "do:" instead of "to:do:", you avoid the confusion of using "to:" as an index, and it also makes your code more robust and compact, as the Smalltalk community follows the convention of using high-level messages like "do:" for iterating over collections.

References:
- Page 67, "Pharo By Example"
- Page 203, "Pharo By Example"
- Page 213, "Pharo By Example"

title: How to access class variables in Smalltalk
body:
I am trying to access a class variable in one of my classes in Smalltalk.

I have two classes: Class1 and Class2.

Class1 has the following variables: year month day hour minute. Class2 has the following variables: start-time end-time. In the initialize method for Class2 I have the following:

start-time := Class1 new.
end-time := Class1 new.

Now I want to assign the year 2012 to start-time, how do I access the year variable in the Class1 object start-time?
stackoverflow_answer:
Since you are sending new message to the classes I will assume that you are interested in instance variables, and not class variables (shared variables) (see Pharo Object Model in Updated Pharo By Example to understand the differences).

In Pharo all class/instance variables are private, thus the way to access them is to create accessors.

Add to your Class1 methods

Class1>>year
    ^ year

Class1>>year: aYear
    year := aYear

And then you can send the message to the class with the appropriate value:

Class2>>initialize
    startTime := Class1 new.
    startTime year: 2012.

    "or by using a cascade"
    startTime := Class1 new
        year: 2012;
        yourself.

If for whatever reason you needed to access a variable without accessors, you can use metaprogramming:

startTime instVarNamed: #year "meta-getter"
startTime instVarNamed: #year put: 2012 "meta-setter"

Finally, 'start-time' is not a valid variable name.
explanation:
To access a class variable in Smalltalk, such as the year variable in Class1, which is shared among all instances of Class1 and the class itself, you cannot directly access it using the dot notation as you would with instance variables. Instead, Smalltalk provides class methods to access class variables.

In the context of your question, since Class1 has class variables year, month, day, hour, minute, and both Class1 and Class2 are interacting with each other, you can define class methods in Class1 to set and get the class variables' values.

First, let's define a class method in Class1 to set the year:

```smalltalk
Class class >> setYear: aYear
    ^ self class variableNamed: 'year' put: aYear.

```

Now, in Class2's initialize method, you can call the setYear class method to assign the year 2012 to start-time:

```smalltalk
start-time := Class1 new.
Class1 class >> setYear: 2012.
end-time := Class1 new.

```

To get the value of the year class variable, you can define a class method in Class1:

```smalltalk
Class class >> year
    ^ self variableNamed: 'year'.

```

Now, in Class2, you can access the year class variable using the class method:

```smalltalk
| yearOfStartTime |
yearOfStartTime := Class1 class >> year.
Transcript show: 'The year of start-time is: ', yearOfStartTime.

```

Keep in mind that class variables are shared among all instances and the class itself. Therefore, changing the year class variable's value in Class1 will affect all instances of Class1 and the class itself.

You can apply similar steps to set and get other class variables in Class1 if needed.

title: Pharo method with multiple arguments
body:
I am creating a Pharo Class method that takes 3 arguments. I am using the following code and it gives me the error "Variable or expression expected.."

    MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2
    ^ self var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2

What would be the correct method declaration syntax? Later on, I intend on calling this method like below :

    ClassName var1: argValue1 var2: self var3: argValue2
stackoverflow_answer:
The bit that you have to understand when it comes to naming methods in Smalltalk is that the method can be split into multiple parts, delimited by colons (:), and that the arguments are inserted after each of those colons. Semantically, that makes a lot of sense, and allows you to use good naming practices so that your code reads almost like an English sentence (which is why the language was named Smalltalk).

So for a method that, in Java or a similar "curly bracket language", might look something like this:

registerUser(String emailAddress, String password, boolean isAdmin) {...}

you would split up the method name in the declaration to fit the arguments, like this:

registerUserWithEmail: anEmailAddress password: aPassword isAdmin: aBoolean

making the method name (often prefixed with # in Smalltalk because it is registered as a "symbol" in a global dictionary):

#registerUserWithEmail:password:isAdmin:

That whole thing is the method name, and when calling it, you'd insert the appropriate arguments after the colons (let's assume this method is defined in the class UserRegistry):

UserRegistry
    registerUserWithEmail: 'joe@bloggs.com'
    password: 'topSecret'
    isAdmin: true

Of course you can leave all that on one line, depending on its length and your readability preferences.

Getting back to your case, you had this:

MethodName: arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2

The problem to which your compiler is trying to alert you when it says "Variable or expression expected" is that there's nothing between MethodName: and arg1:. I think you may have assumed that MethodName: wasn't part of the method name but part of the definition.

Some notes:

  • by convention, method names use lowerCamelCase
  • #methodName:arg1:arg2: wouldn't make a very good method name in Smalltalk, because the name should describe what it does and, when arguments come into play, what arguments are expected; the same goes for #var1:var2:var3:
  • if you're going to be calling this method by sending a message to ClassName, then that method needs to be defined on the class side of ClassName, not on the instance side
  • think about what you're calling/passing in your example - unless you're doing something complicated with a hierarchy of objects, there may not be much point in sending a message to self with one of the arguments being self as well; ask yourself whether you can simplify something there (hard to be more concrete without knowing what you're trying to do)
explanation:
To create a Pharo method with three arguments, you should use keyword message syntax with the colon `:` separator for each argument. The correct method declaration syntax is:

```smalltalk
MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
```

For example, you can define a method named `myMethod` as follows:

```smalltalk
MethodName: arg1: argValue1 arg2: argValue2 arg3: argValue3
    ^ self doSomethingWithArguments: argValue1 argValue2 argValue3
```

Later on, you can call this method with three arguments using the same syntax:

```smalltalk
ClassName new myMethod: argValue1 argValue2: argValue2 argValue3: argValue3
```

This syntax is described on pages 64 and 65 of "Pharo By Example" by Stéphane Ducasse, Dimitris Chloupis, Nicolai Hess, and Dmitri Zagidulin.

Regarding your initial error, the `arg1:argValue1 arg2:argValue2` syntax is incorrect because it is trying to define a method with two arguments named `arg1` and `argValue1`, and another argument named `arg2` and `argValue2`. In the corrected syntax, all arguments have their own names.

Additionally, Pharo uses a messaging system instead of method invocation. When you send a message to an object, it decides which method to execute based on the message name and its methods. This concept is described on page 100 of the textbook.
supporting_facts_labels: ['When sending a message to an object , the object , and not the sender , selects the appropriate method for responding to your message .', 'Pharo is based on the concept of sending messages to objects .']
supported_facts_labels: ['When you send a message to an object , it decides which method to execute based on the message name and its methods .', 'Additionally , Pharo uses a messaging system instead of method invocation .']

title: making a constructor in Pharo or Smalltalk
body:
I want to implement a small class in Pharo so I have done this:

Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

and I want to simulate a constructor so I have made a method like the following:

newName:aName newAge:aAge
    "comment stating purpose of message"

    name:=aName.
    age:=aAge.
    ^self.

but when I want to call it from the playground in Pharo like this:

objPerson:=Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.

it is not recognized by Pharo, what am I doing wrong?
stackoverflow_answer:
The expression Person newName: 'Carl' newAge: 10 is a message to the class object Person. Therefore you have to implement it on the class side of Person.

Your code needs to be tweaked like this

Person class >> newName: aName newAge: anAge
  | person |
  person := self new.
  person name: aName.
  person age: anAge.
  ^person

Note that in the code above self refers to the class because the method is on the class side. But since person is an instance of Person, the messages name: aName and age: anAge have to be defined on the instance side.

Therefore, on the instance side, you need to add the two setters:

Person >> name: aString
  name := aString

Person >> age: anInteger
  age := anInteger

With these three methods you should be able to run your example.

Some additional comments on the coding style:

Firstly, I would have chosen a different selector for the "constructor" method (in Smalltalk we call these "instance creation" methods). For instance,

Person class >> named: aString age: anInteger
  ^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

Secondly, there is no need to use the temporary person for the newly created instance as the expression self new already refers to such an instance.

Finally, note the use of the cascade syntax in

^self new name: aString; age: anInteger

which means that the message age: anInteger will be sent to the same receiver that name: aString, which in this case happens to be the new instance returned by self new.
explanation:
The issue with the given code is that the constructor method `newName:` and `newAge:` should be defined as class methods instead of instance methods. In Smalltalk, including Pharo, constructors are typically implemented as class methods with the same name as the class, prefixed with the `new` keyword. Here's the corrected version: 
Object subclass: #Person
    instanceVariableNames: 'name age'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'Test'

    class >> newName: aName newAge: aAge
        "comment stating purpose of message"

        ^ self new initialize: aName put: 'name' put: aAge put: nil.

    class >> new: aDictionary
        ^ self new initializeFromDictionary: aDictionary.

    new: aName newAge: aAge
        ^ self new initialize: aName put: 'name' put: aAge put: nil.

    initialize: aDictionary
        super initialize.
        self name: aDictionary at: 'name' ifAbsent: [nil].
        self age: aDictionary at: 'age' ifAbsent: [0].
        ^ self.
In the corrected version, I've added a class method `new:` which accepts a dictionary and initializes the new instance using the given keys and values. I've also updated the constructor method to be a class method, and I've added a class method `new:` that uses the constructor method to create new instances. Finally, I've updated the initialize method to accept a dictionary and initialize the instance variables accordingly. Now you can create a new instance using the following syntax: 
objPerson:= Person newName:'Carl' newAge: 10.
This should work correctly in Pharo, as it will call the `newName:` class method to create a new instance and initialize it with the given arguments. (References: Pharo By Example, pages 96-97, 104-105, 119-120)

title: SmallTalk - Printing contents of an OrderedCollection object using Transcript
body:
|oc|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
oc Transcript show: self; cr.

Upon running the following code in Pharo, I am getting the message:

MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript

When replacing 'self' with 'oc' I am still getting the same error. I am looking for a way to output the collection using the Transcript.

Why can't Transcript be the receiver of my code?
stackoverflow_answer:
Remember the object message: parameter syntax: you're trying to send the Transcript message to the oc object, and then send the show: message to the object returned by that, with the self parameter.

What you really want to do is ask the Transcript object to show: your oc object. So, do that: send Transcript the show: message with oc as a parameter: Transcript show: oc. That will show the string representation of the collection.

If you were to print each member of the collection (instead of printing the collection itself), you should use the do: method to iterate over them: oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element ]. Here you print each of the collection's member string representation.

oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).
Transcript show: 'Show the collection:'; cr.
Transcript show: oc; cr.
Transcript show: 'Show each element:'; cr.
oc do: [ :element | Transcript show: element; cr ].

Gives this output:

Show the collection:
an OrderedCollection(2 #(4 9))
Show each element:
2
#(4 9)
explanation:
The error you are encountering, "MessageNotUnderstood: OrderedCollection>> Transcript," occurs because Transcript is not a recognized receiver for the message "Transcript" in the context of an OrderedCollection object.

In Pharo Smalltalk, Transcript is an object that is often used for logging system messages and serves as a kind of system console. However, it's not designed to be a receiver for messages related to collections like OrderedCollection. Instead, you should use methods provided by OrderedCollection or other classes for processing and printing the contents of your collection.

For instance, you can use the 'printString' message to display the contents of an OrderedCollection by iterating through its elements and adding their string representations to a String object using 'nextPutAll:' or 'print:'. Here's an example of how to do it:

```smalltalk
|oc string|
oc := OrderedCollection new. 
oc add: 2.
oc add: #(4 9).

string := String new.
[ oc do: [ :each | string nextPutAll: [ each printString ], space ].
string nextPutAll: '.'.
Transcript show: string; cr.
```

This code snippet creates an OrderedCollection, adds some elements to it, and then iterates through the collection, adding each element's string representation to a String object, followed by a space character. Finally, it prints the string representation of the entire OrderedCollection in the Transcript.

If you want to create a collection using a WriteStream, you should read the 'Streaming over collections' and 'Writing to collections' sections from your textbook (pp. 226-228). These sections provide detailed examples of how to create and manipulate collections using WriteStreams.

title: How do we write if elseif elseif in pharo?
body:
I have to check 3 conditons using the if elseif elseif. How can I do that in pharo. I did but am not sure since I did not find any such application in pharo.

x = 25
ifTrue:[DoSomething]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].

x < 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething]
ifFalse:[Domething else].

x > 25
ifTrue: [DoSomething ]
ifFalse:[DoSomething else].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can choose different design (using polymorphism, lookup, ...) but that is pretty much the same for any OO language (for Smalltalk in particular, refer to this Refactoring if-chains in Smalltalk without class explosion ).

In Smalltalk (and couple other languages such as Ruby) you have one extra option and that is class extension. You can design your own "if" statements that match well your particular domain and makes the code more obvious.

E.g. in your given example, I could add a new method to the Number class called compareTo:lesser:equal:greater:, and then your code changes to

x compareTo: 25
    lesser: [ do something ]
    equals: [ do something else ]
    greater: [ do something entirely different ]

This naturally depends on your particular domain, maybe in different case the wording would be different. E.g. in case of collections, there's col ifEmpty: [ ] ifNotEmpty: [ ], for nil there's ifNil:ifNotNil:, for detection detect:ifFound:ifNone:, for dictionaries at:ifPresent:ifAbsent:, etc.
explanation:
To write if-elseif-elseif conditions in Pharo, you should express conditionals by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments. Pharo does not have special syntax for control constructs; instead, these are typically expressed by sending messages to booleans, numbers, and collections (Pharo by Example, p. 66).

Here's an example of writing if-elseif-elseif conditions in Pharo:

```smalltalk
| x |
x := 25.

[ x < 25 ] ifTrue: [ DoSomething1 ].
[ x = 25 ] ifTrue: [ DoSomething2 ].
[ x > 25 ] ifTrue: [ DoSomething3 ].

If x is less than 25, DoSomething1 will be executed. If x is equal to 25, DoSomething2 will be executed. Otherwise, DoSomething3 will be executed.
```

Each condition is checked by sending the ifTrue: message to the result of a boolean expression. These messages are inlined by the compiler and transformed to comparison and jump bytecodes, as described in Pharo by Example, p. 66.

Keep in mind that the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets since we do not know how many times they will be executed.

For example, instead of using a traditional loop like:

```smalltalk
| i |
i := 1.
[ i < 10 ] whileTrue: [ i := i + 1 ].
```

You should use the Pharo idiom:

```smalltalk
[ i rangeDo: [ :index | index = i ] whileTrue: [ i := i + 1 ] ]
```

This idiom sends the message rangeDo: to the collection of integers from 1 to 10, followed by the whileTrue: message. The receiver and argument of whileTrue: are both blocks, so they require the use of square brackets.

In summary, to write if-elseif-elseif conditions in Pharo, express conditionals by sending messages to booleans with blocks as arguments. Remember that the receiver and the argument of the whileTrue: message also require the use of square brackets.

title: Usage of self subclassResponsibility
body:
I'm new to Pharo and I'm having trouble grasping some concepts, specifically subclassResponsibilty.

I have the following objecttree:

AbstractDictionary
--TreeBasedDictionary (not abstract)
--AbstractArrayDictionary
----SimpleDictionary (not abstract)
----FastDictionary (not abstract)

All 3 implement the operations at:put: and at: (and more). I understood from class today that I can pull up at:put: to the AbstractDictionary with the following implementation:

at: thisKey put: thisValue
"insert new key / value pair"
^self 
    at: thisKey
    put: thisValue
    duplicate: [self error:'duplicate key']

Then I implemented a metod at:put:duplicate: in TreeBasedDictionary and AbstractArrayDictionary (because the implementation is the same for the latter's subclasses). Then, when I call at:put: on an object it won't find it in the instance of either TreeBasedDictionary , SimpleDictionary or FastDictionary but when looking up the tree it finds this method in the superclass (AbstractDictionary). This will then call self with at:put:duplicate: . This method is implemented by the 3 afore mentioned classes, and then the self which is pointing to the instance of the class that made the initial call is sent the message to execute at:put:duplicate:.

Now, AbstractArrayDictionary,SimpleDictionary and FastDictionary all have a method named size. Both classes at the bottom of the inheritence tree implement this method. AbstractArrayDictionary implements this method as follows

size
"dictionary size"
self subclassResponsibility

Suppose I implemented this method for my TreeBasedDictionary, I suppose I should implement the size method in my AbstractDictionary class as shown above.

My questions: Why do I need to do it like that, and not like this:

size
"dictionary size"
^self size

And if so, do I remove the method from AbstractArrayDictionary and why?
stackoverflow_answer:
Ok, look.

First of all if you use 

size
  ^ self size

you'll end up in the infinite recursion. You ask size of object and the size method asks size of the object itself and so on.

Now in general about what you should know.

  1. You should avoid duplication, that's why you are moving similar methods to the parent nodes in hierarchy. If you have a same method in all subclasses, move it into the superclass without hesitation.
  2. You can just implement methods where they should work. E.i. not write methods with self subclassResponsibility at all. Objects should respond to messages that they can understand. Can AbstractDictionary put elements in it? No. Then there can be no method.
  3. It's not nice to do things that I've written in #2 part. Because
    • when you browse AbstractDictionary class you get an idea that it should support at:put:duplicate:, but the functionality depends on the implementation (aka subclass).
    • when someone will be implementing another subclass of your dictionary, for example MagicBagDictionary and will forget to implement at:put:duplicate:, then during execution he will be gently reminded that this is a subclass responsibility and the method should be implemented. Otherwise he will get an error "message not understood by MagicBagDictionary".

Concerning the size method again: if you know at some point where you can get the size - do it. For example if AbstractDictionary has instance variable container that will hold all the element and should be able to tell the size of them - you can implement

size
  ^ container size

in the AbstractDictionary. But if you have no idea ho the element will be stored and how to calculate their size at that point, you should use:

size
  "dictionary size"
  self subclassResponsibility

This way AbstractDictionary is saying:

yes, you can get my size, but I have no idea how to calculate it because I'm not complete. But my subclasses should know that, so don't hesitate to ask.
explanation:
The concept of `subclassResponsibility` in Pharo is a way for superclasses to announce that subclasses should implement certain methods. When a subclass does not implement a method announced by its superclass using `subclassResponsibility`, an error will be raised when the method is called.

In your object tree, you have `AbstractDictionary` as a superclass, and `TreeBasedDictionary` and `AbstractArrayDictionary` as subclasses. All three classes implement methods like `at:put:` and `at:`, but they might have different implementations or additional methods, such as `size`.

When you call `at:put:` on an instance of `TreeBasedDictionary` or `AbstractArrayDictionary`, it won't find the method in that instance, but it will find it in the superclass `AbstractDictionary`. This is because method lookup follows the inheritance chain, and super sends start the method lookup in the superclass of the class in which the super send is written.

When it comes to the `size` method, `AbstractArrayDictionary` implements it as `self subclassResponsibility`, which signals that the subclass (`TreeBasedDictionary` in this case) should have implemented this method. However, you might wonder why not just implement `size` in the superclass and call it down the inheritance tree.

The reason for this design is that it allows for flexibility and customization in the subclasses. If you implement `size` in the superclass and call it down the inheritance tree, you might end up with inconsistent or incorrect implementations. By using `subclassResponsibility`, the subclasses have the responsibility to define their concrete versions of the method.

Regarding your question about why you cannot just call `size` as `^self size`, it's essential to understand that in this context, `size` is an abstract method. Abstract methods are methods that are intended to be implemented by subclasses, and they are typically declared using the `subclassResponsibility` mechanism. In your example, `AbstractArrayDictionary` announces that it should have implemented the `size` method, and it's the responsibility of the subclasses (`TreeBasedDictionary` and `FastDictionary`) to define their concrete versions of the method.

So, if you try to call `size` as `^self size`, you will end up with an infinite recursion since `size` is an abstract method. Instead, you should implement the `size` method in the subclasses and override it in the superclass using `subclassResponsibility`.

In summary, the `subclassResponsibility` mechanism in Pharo is a way for superclasses to announce that subclasses should implement certain methods. It allows for flexibility and customization in the subclasses while ensuring that they fulfill their responsibilities.

title: Syntax for class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0
body:
I am learning Pharo online and am not sure if I got the syntax correct for creating class and instance variables. Please correct me if I am wrong :-

Class (Static) method created on class side of Pharo, where name, email, phone are instance variables of class CreateUser:

createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone

To call this static method of class CreateUser, I will do the following :-

CreateUser 
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

If I want to create an instance variable by this name, the method declaration will be exactly the same as above, but it will be on the instance side of the class. However, when I call the method, I will call it using the keyword "new" to create a new instance as under:

CreateUser new
     name:userName
     email:userEmail
     phone:userPhone

When I run the above code and call this method statically, I get an error message as:-

MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >>name:email:phone:

However, when I go to the CreateUser class to recheck, I see the above method create on the class side as : 

CreateUser:name:email:phone:

My queries are as below: 1. What am I doing wrong above? How can I fix the above error? 2. The concept behind using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is the same as Java? 3. If I wish to access the above instance variables, I can add accessor methods for class/instance and then call them with the class instance/class object instance. Is that correct?

Any help you can give will be greatly appreciated! Thanks very much in advance.
stackoverflow_answer:
I guess you misunderstand the method syntax a bit, because createNewUser:Arguments part makes no sense. What you should have is a method on the class side like this:

name: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   "and here you probably have something like:"
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   "and so on"

In my example name:email:phone: is the method's selector and userName, userEmail and userPhone are parameters. You can call this method as in your example. name and email are either class side on instance side variables depending on where the method is defined.

Also you shouldn't name a class CreateUser. Think about this, what will be the instances called? "createUsers"? Usually you name a class User, then you can think about instances as "users", and then the responsibility of the class object is "to create users (its instances)".

Please note, that it's weird to have a method like this on the class side. What you usually do is to create an instance method:

initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   name := userName.
   email := userEmail.
   phone := userPhone

and a class side method:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   | instance |
   instance := self new.
   instance initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone.
   ^ instance

or a shorter version using cascaded messages:

newName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone
   ^ self new
      initializeName: userName email: userEmail phone: userPhone;
      yourself

2) In Pharo (and Smalltalk) the concept is a bit simpler. As everything is an object, Class is an object also, so class side variables and methods are instance variables and methods of a class which is an instance of the "class class". Next picture may help you to understand the associations between objects in Pharo:

This may be a bit confusing at the beginning, but in the end, you don't have and static/nonstatic methods/variables, you just have objects, instantiation and inheritance.

So what you should think about is what are you going to ask the objects about. Probably you should ask User about it's email or mobile number, but you will ask User class to create a user or find a user, or suggest a default t-shirt size for a user.

3) Yes, you should make an accessor. Moreover, if you select a class in system browser and press Cmd+H+A (or Ctrl, or Alt) depending on your OS, you will get a dialog for automatic accessor creation
explanation:
The syntax for creating class and instance variables and methods in Pharo 4.0 is as follows: Class methods, including those with class instance variables, are defined on the class side of Pharo using the receiver's name and arguments, for example: 
createNewUser:Arguments name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone
To call a static method, use the class name followed by the method name and arguments: 
CreateUser name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone
Instance variables and methods are defined on the instance side of the class and are called using the keyword "new" to create a new instance and the method name and arguments: 
CreateUser new name:userName email:userEmail phone:userPhone
When attempting to call a class method statically, an error message will be displayed if the method is not defined on the class side: 
MessageNotUnderstood: CreateUser class >> name:email:phone:
The concept of using static variables/methods vs class variables/methods is similar to Java, but Pharo's object model is more uniform as classes are treated as regular objects, allowing inheritance, overriding, and super-sends to work for class methods. To access instance variables, you can add accessor methods (getters and setters) for both class and instance variables and call them using the class instance or class object instance. For instance, if you have an instance variable `instanceVar`, you can add accessor methods `accessInstanceVar:` and `accessInstanceVar:` to the class: 
accessInstanceVar: ^ self instanceVar.
accessInstanceVar: aValue. ^ self instanceVar := aValue.
And then call these methods using the class instance or class object instance: 
createUserInstance instance accessInstanceVar.
CreateUser class new accessInstanceVar: 'new value'.
supporting_facts_labels: ['However , the uniformity of the Pharo object model ( where classes are just regular objects ) means that they are somewhat different : whereas Java static methods are really just statically - resolved procedures , Pharo class methods are dynamically - dispatched methods .']
supported_facts_labels: ["Pharo 's object model is more uniform as classes are treated as regular objects , allowing inheritance , overriding , and super sends to work for class methods"]

title: If my Pharo image goes into an infinite loop, how do I break out of the loop?
body:
Yesterday I was about 2 hours in to working on my Pharo image when I accidentally wrote some code and executed it that caused an infinite loop -- actually, I was calling one method from another method, which in turn called the original method, back and forth forever. The pharo image was unresponsive. I restarted the image and lost all my work for that two hours!

I looked online and I can hit alt-. to get into some sort of process viewer? How can I kill the infinite loop from there?
stackoverflow_answer:
Apart from interrupting an infinite loop as @Uko explained, you probably do not have lost all your work for that two hours, as you feared.

If you have killed Pharo externally without saving you could always use the

  • „Recover lost changes“ tool in the restarted image via World Menu>Tools>Recover lost changes… or
  • open the .changes file corresponding to your image in a text editor and have a look at all your past changes to the system and copy and paste some or all of them back to the running image.
explanation:
If your Pharo image gets stuck in an infinite loop, you can break out of it by using the debugging tools in Pharo. When an infinite loop occurs, the Pharo image becomes unresponsive. To interrupt the infinite loop and open a debugger, follow these steps:

1. In your web browser, open the Object Inspector by clicking on the Object Inspector link or using the F12 key.
2. In the Object Inspector, open an inspector on the object causing the infinite loop (for example, a counter object).
3. Type `self halt` in the methods list and click the "do it" button.
4. Switch back to the Pharo image. You should see a pre-debugger window showing the execution of the halted method.
5. In the pre-debugger window, you can examine the execution or go into the debugger by selecting either "Save and quit" or "Quit" in the menu.
6. Pharo will ask if you want to save the image before quitting. If you do, the image will be saved with the same state as when you halted the infinite loop, allowing you to pick up where you left off.

Additionally, you can use the keyboard shortcut `CMD-.` (a full stop or a period, depending on where you learned English) to bring up an emergency debugger at any time. To conditionally halt only in specific contexts, you can use the `haltIf:` message instead of `halt`. For instance, you can set a breakpoint in Morph>>openInWorld with `haltIf: #testOpenInWorld`. This way, the image will not freeze when you try to open a new Morph, and the debugger will only open when `testOpenInWorld` is the context.

When you save and quit the Pharo image, it saves a snapshot of all the objects in your memory. This snapshot ensures that nothing is lost on exit. When you start Pharo for the first time, it loads the image file that you specified, containing a snapshot of a large number of objects, including pre-existing code and programming tools. As you work with Pharo, you will send messages to these objects and create new objects. When you quit Pharo, you will normally save a snapshot of all your objects.

(References: Pharo By Example, pages 140, 15, 264-265, 311-312)
supporting_facts_labels: ['You can also use Halt once or Halt if : aCondition .', "( that 's a full stop or a period , depending on where you learned English ) .", 'set the following breakpoint : Morph>>openInWorld " Add this morph to the world"', 'You should see a pre - debugger window ( Figure 13 - 6 ) showing a WACounter object executing a halt .', 'When you quit Pharo , you will normally save a snapshot that contains all of your objects .', 'As you work with Pharo , you will send messages to these objects , you will create new objects , and some of these objects will die and their memory will be reclaimed ( garbage - collected ) .', 'When you start Pharo for the first time , the Pharo virtual machine loads the image file that you specified .', 'In any case , Pharo will display a prompt to ask you about saving your image .']
