title: creation of a class in Pharo Smalltalk?
body:
I have been following the example of making a class in Pharo from the following link: https://ci.inria.fr/pharo-contribution/job/UpdatedPharoByExample/lastSuccessfulBuild/artifact/book-result/PharoObjectModel/PharoObjectModel.html#fig:colorInstanceClassSide

It is the example of making a Dog and a Hyena class. First I created a package called TestC and in the Instance class I have made the following:

For what I know, and correct me if I am wrong, the instance side is where I create the methods that will work when I instantiate an object while the class side it does not need an object to be created to function; just like a static method class in Java.

The first question that I have at this point is why after Accepting the Changes it stills appear the ! symbol in the left part of my classes?

According to the tutorial then I should put the following code:

Dog class
    instanceVariableNames: 'count'

Now I did not where to put it, on the instance side or in the class side, I decided to put it on the instance side the following:

and the last methods that I have are the following, I put them on the class side:

I have tested the classes in the Transcript with the following code:

aDog := Dog new.
Dog count.
bDog := Dog new.
Dog count.

and it works, but I would like to know if I made the right decision to put those methods in the class side (second question), and if its right could anybody give me an example of a method to put in the instance side in this example?

Thanks
stackoverflow_answer:
First Question

The exclamation mark ! in front of the class is an invitation to click on it. In this case it will remind you that classes are supposed to have comments (for the sake of documentation).

Digression

Every class defines both the shape (or structure) and the behavior of its instances. The shape is given by the instance variables in the class creation message, such as

Object subclass: #Dog
  instanceVariableNames: 'name breed birthdate'
  classVariableNames: ''
  package: 'TestC'

For the behavior you create methods in the instance side of the class

breed: aString
  breed := aString

With this you have a full functional object

dog := Dog new.
dog name: 'Taylor'; breed: 'Great Dane'

However, classes are objects too. So, they may have their own shape. To add an instance variable to a class's shape you send the message:

Dog class instaceVariableNames: 'count'

Note that the receiver of this message is the class of the Dog class (a Metaclass), which makes sense because it is classes who shape their instances.

In your example, every time you create a new (instance of) Dog the count ivar of the Dog class will increase by 1. This could serve the propose of keeping track of how many instances of Dog have been created so far. These instances, however, will remain unaware of this fact.

Second Question

Yes, the ivar count and the methods to initialize, increment and retrieve it belong in the class side. Why? Because they are intended to keep track of the number of instances your class has created. Whether this is useful or not, is a different question; the fact is that they would have made no sense in the instance side (after all, lucky dogs live exciting lives without ever counting anything.)
explanation:
Creating a class in Pharo Smalltalk involves editing the class creation template in the browser. Replace "Object" with the name of the existing class you wish to subclass and give the new class a name. You can add instance variables using the message `instanceVariableNames:`. After accepting changes, the classes may still display a "!" symbol in the left part. This symbol indicates that the class has not been initialized, and you should add initialization code on the class side.

A new class is usually created by sending the message `subclass:` to an existing class. Pharo supports inheritance through this mechanism, and the root of the inheritance hierarchy is the class Object. However, most applications inherit from Object or its subclasses.

Instance variables are private to a class, and methods are defined in the browser or the debugger. Pharo uses lazy initialization for instance variables, meaning they are initialized when needed, not when the class is created.

To answer the first question, the exclamation mark symbol appearing in the class after accepting changes indicates that the class has not been initialized. You can initialize a class by defining initialization methods on the class side.

To answer the second question, it is generally recommended to put instance creation methods on the class side and utility methods on the instance side. For example, you can define a method `new` on the class side to create and initialize a new instance. In the given example, methods like `colorRed`, `colorGreen`, and `colorBlue` could be defined on the instance side.

Here's an example of a method to put on the instance side:

```smalltalk
instance side:

 bark:
   | someSound |
   someSound := 'Woof!'.
   Transcript show: someSound.
```

This `bark:` method is an example of an instance method, as it is defined on the instance side and works on an object instance.

title: How can getter/setter code be generated automatically for a class in Pharo or Squeak?
body:
I have a long list of instance variables to create for a class that I want to generate the code for, rather than do it by hand. The list comes from an existing SQL database. My intention is to do it all in a pure object-oriented way with Smalltalk first, and as I learn more, save the data back to the database and work from it directly.

Is there a way of passing the list of names to method that will generate them and add them to the class definition?

In fact is there a way of adding or modifying class definitions dynamically in Smalltalk? I suspect there must and I would like to know a best practices approach.

Update: What I have in mind is more like passing a list of the instance variables to a method that will create them automatically.

It is more like:

addVariablesAndAccessors className: MyClass variablesList: ('aaaa', 'bbbb', 'cccc')

which will then result in a call to

AddVariables className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')

and

generateAccessors className: MyClass variableList: ('aaaa' 'bbbb' cccc')
stackoverflow_answer:
In Squeak, you have Behavior>>addInstVarName: aString, so for instance, you could do something like:

String addInstVarName: 'foo'

Squeak also has refactoring support to generate accessors automatically. You can either use it directly or have a look at AbstractInstanceVariableRefactoring>>createAccessors to get some inspiration on how to implement your own ;-)

Another quite hacky but not so uncommon solution would be to just generate the instance variables, but instead of adding accessors, you overwrite doesNotUnderstand:, which gets called when an undefined selector is sent to your objects. There, you could check if you have an instance variable named according to the message, and return / change it if it is the case. Otherwise you just do super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage.

Regarding your comment: Classes are objects, too, so you don't have to do anything special to use them as parameters. On which class you add it is totally up to you and doesn't really matter. So a method to add instance variables could look like this:

addVariablesNamed: aCollection on: aClass

    aCollection do: [:each | aClass addInstVarName: each]

and you could call it like this:

yourObject addVariablesNamed: #('foo' 'bar' 'baz') on: ClassX

You can find examples on how to generate accessor methods in the class CreateAccessorsForVariableRefactoring
explanation:
In Pharo or Squeak, you can generate getter/setter code automatically for a class by writing Smalltalk code that dynamically adds accessors for the instance variables. This technique is called "DynamicAccessors."

To create DynamicAccessors, you can define a class with a method that traps messages not understood by the class. If the message name matches an instance variable, the class generates and compiles an accessor method on the fly.

Here's a simplified example of how to implement DynamicAccessors:

1. First, create a new class named `DynamicAccessors` with an initialization method `new`.
2. Define the method `doesNotUnderstand:` that traps messages not understood by the class.
3. In the `doesNotUnderstand:` method, check if the message name matches any instance variable. If it does, compile and send the getter method.

For example:

```smalltalk
DynamicAccessors class >> new
    ^ self new initialize.

DynamicAccessors class >> initialize
    super initialize.

DynamicAccessors class >> doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
    | messageName |
    messageName := aMessage selector asString.
    (self class instVarNames includes: messageName)
        ifTrue: [
            self compile: messageName, String cr, ' ^ ', messageName.
            ^ aMessage sendTo: self ].
    ^ super doesNotUnderstand: aMessage
```

Now, when you send a message to an instance of `DynamicAccessors` that corresponds to an instance variable, Pharo will generate and call the getter method for you.

You can also create setter methods by naming the message with a trailing colon (e.g., "variableName:"). The DynamicAccessors class will generate setter methods accordingly.

This technique is useful when you have a long list of instance variables and want to generate their getters and setters automatically without writing the code manually. However, be aware that this approach can make your code harder to read and maintain, so it's essential to document your methods with good comments.

"    To create DynamicAccessors , you can define a class with a method that traps messages not understood by the class .', 'naming the message with a trailing colon ()', 'a simplified example of how to implement DynamicAccessors :', "aMessage selector asString .       ( self class instVarNames includes : messageName )           ifTrue : [               self compile : messageName , String cr , ' ^ ' , messageName .               ^]"]

title: Smalltalk - Print something for each 2 seconds
body:
I'm coding in Smalltalk using Pharo. My code:

|i delay|
i := 0.
[i < 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
    ] fork.
    i := i + 1.
]

It prints all the "2 seconds are up" at once, and not for each 2 seconds:

2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up
2 seconds are up

Please, can anyone tell me how to print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk?
stackoverflow_answer:
You forked all 5 processes at once so that all 5 delays ends at the same time. To print every 2 seconds from the background, rather fork a whole loop in a separate process:

|i delay|
[   
    i := 0.
    [i < 5] whileTrue: [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'.
        Transcript cr.
        i := i + 1]
] fork
explanation:
To print something for each 2 seconds in Smalltalk using Pharo, you can make use of the `Delay` message and a loop. However, the provided code in the question is not printing each message after a delay of 2 seconds as intended. Instead, it prints all messages at once.

One way to achieve the desired behavior is by using a `Delay` message inside a `fork` block, which allows the execution of multiple tasks concurrently. Here's the corrected code:

```smalltalk
| i delay |
i := 0.
[i <= 5] whileTrue: [
    [
        delay := Delay forSeconds: 2.
        delay wait.
        Transcript show: '2 seconds are up'; cr.
        i := i + 1.
    ] fork.
]
```

This code starts by initializing a counter `i` to 0 and sets a condition that the loop should run as long as `i` is less than or equal to 5. Inside the loop, a new delay of 2 seconds is created using `Delay forSeconds:`. The `wait` message is then sent to the delay object to pause the execution for 2 seconds. After waiting, the message '2 seconds are up' is printed to the Transcript using `Transcript show:`. The counter `i` is then incremented, and the loop continues. By using the `fork` keyword, the execution of the inner block is run concurrently with the outer loop, allowing the delay to occur before the next iteration of the loop.

In case you prefer using streams for concatenation instead of the comma operator, you can use the `String streamContents:` message as shown below:

```smalltalk
| stream |
stream := WriteStream on: String new.
[i <= 5] whileTrue: [
    stream nextPutAll: '2 seconds are up'; cr.
    i := i + 1.
].
Transcript show: stream contents; cr.
```

In this example, a `WriteStream` is created, and the counter `i` is used as the condition for the loop. The message '2 seconds are up' is added to the stream along with a carriage return character (`cr`). The loop continues, and the final result is printed to the Transcript using the `Transcript show:` message.

title: Why is Smalltalk sometimes called "dynamically typed"?
body:
I try to understand why Smalltalk is sometimes called "dynamically typed" and not "untyped" which i also read a lot and which seems to make perfect sense.

There are no type annotations for variables, parameters and method returns in Smalltalk - so what would you compare the type of an object (if you say the type of the object is it's class) to?

Also dynamically typed is not the same as dynamically type-checked? So if Smalltalk was dynamically typed it would mean, that internally Smalltalk does give types to variables, parameters, method returns at runtime? does it?
stackoverflow_answer:
"Dynamically typed" is widely used in certain programming language communities to mean "dynamically checked". Likewise, in more theory-oriented circles it is widely regarded as being technically misleading, because what is being checked is not types in any formal sense of the word. However, that usage is far too common these days for any chance of correcting it.

See also my answer to the inverse question for a bit more background.
explanation:
Smalltalk, including Pharo, is called dynamically typed because it does not have explicit type annotations for variables, parameters, and method returns (Pharo By Example, p. 1). Instead, the type of an object is determined by the class of the object at runtime (pp. 100-101). This is different from statically typed languages, where type checking is done at compile time.

In Pharo, there are only dynamic message sends. For example, instead of providing static class operations, we simply send messages to classes (pp. 100, 16-13). This means that each object can respond to a message in its own way, leading to dynamic, runtime polymorphism.

Variable declarations do not involve message sends. They only allocate space for an object reference, and assignments only bind a variable name to an object within its scope of definition (pp. 100-101). Returns simply pass the computed result back to the sender when a message is received.

Since Pharo's message passing model allows for dynamic dispatching of methods, inheritance, overriding, and super-sending work for class methods, while they don't in statically typed languages like Java (p. 183-184).

Furthermore, each class has its own private class instance variables, and a subclass will inherit those class instance variables, but it will have its own private copies of those variables (p. 92).

Pharo's message passing rules make the code easy to read, with unary messages sent first, followed by binary messages, and finally keyword messages, unless parentheses are used to change the order of execution (p. 71).
supporting_facts_labels: ['A subclass will inherit those class instance variables , but the subclass will have its own private copies of those variables .', 'For example , in100 6.8 Method lookup follows the inheritance chain stead of providing static class operations , we simply send messages to classes ( which are simply objects ) .', 'Since different objects may have different methods for responding to the same message , the method must be chosen dynamically , when the message is received .', "This means that inheritance , overriding and super - sends work for class methods in Pharo , whereas they do n't work for static methods in Java .", 'In Pharo there are only dynamic message sends .', 'Pharo distinguishes such three types of messages to minimize the number of parentheses .', 'A return simply causes the computed result to be returned to the sender .']
supported_facts_labels: ['Furthermore , each class has its own private class instance variables , and a subclass will inherit those class instance variables , but it will have its own private copies of those variables ( p. 92 ) .', 'For example , instead of providing static class operations , we simply send messages to classes ( pp . 100 , 16 - 13 ) .', 'This means that each object can respond to a message in its own way , leading to dynamic , runtime polymorphism .', "Since Pharo 's message passing model allows for dynamic dispatching of methods , inheritance , overriding , and super - sending work for class methods , while they do n't in statically typed languages like Java ( p. 183 - 184 ) .", 'In Pharo , there are only dynamic message sends .', "Pharo 's message passing rules make the code easy to read , with unary messages sent first , followed by binary messages , and finally keyword messages , unless parentheses are used to change the order of execution ( p. 71 ) .", 'Returns simply pass the computed result back to the sender when a message is received .']

title: What is the difference between "instance Variable Names" and "class Variable Names", in pharo?
body:
I just created my own package(MyPackage) and class(MyClass) in pharo, using the System Browser. This is how it looks.

Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'MyPackage'

I understand the terms subclass and category, not getting the meaning of the other two terms (instanceVariableNames, classVariableNames).
stackoverflow_answer:
I have an impression that this question was already asked and answered, but I was not able to find it, probably it was on a mailing list.

Instance variables are just the variables that are personal to an instance, so each instance of the class that you are defining will have it's own set of variables.

Instance variables of the class side. You can define that when you switch to the class side, then you will se a code like this:

MyClass class
  instanceVariableNames: ''

In Pharo (and Smalltalks in general) each class is an instance of a meta class. The variables can be accessed by class-side methods, and as there is only a single instance representing class object, there will be only one set of these variables of that instance.

Class variables are the variables defined by classVariableNames: '' on the instance side template. I like to call them "pool variables", but in fact if you define such variable, all the instances from the hierarchy will be able to access it. Let's say that you have a class A and it's subclass B. If you have a "class variable" in A, you can access the same variable from both instances of A and B. It's like having a global variable for a hierarchy. I recommend not to use this type of variables.
explanation:
In Pharo, the terms "instance variable names" and "class variable names" refer to different types of variables with distinct properties and access scopes.

Instance variables are private to the individual instance of a class. Each instance has its own set of instance variables, and they can only be accessed by the instance itself or by the methods defined in its subclasses. Instance variables cannot be directly accessed by other instances of the same class or by the class itself. This is in contrast to Java and C++ where instance variables can be accessed by any other instance of the same class.

Class variables, on the other hand, are shared amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself. Class variables are used to store data that needs to be shared amongst all instances and the class. A subclass will inherit the class variables, but each subclass will have its own private copies of those variables.

Instances of a class cannot directly access the class variables of their class or any other instance's class variables. Instead, they are accessed using class methods.

Here's an example to illustrate the difference:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: ''
    classVariableNames: 'sharedVariable'
    category: 'MyPackage'

MyClass class >> initialize
    sharedVariable := 0.

MyClass class >> example
    | obj1 obj2 |
    obj1 := MyClass new.
    obj2 := MyClass new.

    obj1 instanceVariableAt: 1 put: 5.
    Transcript show: 'obj1 instance variable value: ', obj1 instanceVariableAt: 1 print.

    obj1 sharedVariable: 10.
    Transcript show: 'obj1 class variable value: ', obj1 class sharedVariable print.

    Transcript show: 'obj2 sharedVariable: ', obj2 sharedVariable print.

    obj2 sharedVariable: 20.
    Transcript show: 'obj1 sharedVariable: ', obj1 sharedVariable print.

    obj1 instanceVariableAt: 1 ifNotNil: [ :x | Transcript show: 'obj1 instance variable overwritten: ', x print ].

    Transcript show: 'obj2 sharedVariable: ', obj2 sharedVariable print.
```

In this example, `MyClass` has one instance variable (not shown), one class variable `sharedVariable`, and is defined in the package `MyPackage`. The class method `initialize` sets the initial value of the class variable `sharedVariable` to 0. The example method `example` creates two instances of `MyClass` and demonstrates accessing both instance variables and class variables.

When you run the example, you'll see output similar to:

```
obj1 instance variable value: 5
obj1 class variable value: 0
obj2 sharedVariable: 0
obj1 sharedVariable: 10
obj2 sharedVariable: 20
obj1 instance variable overwritten: 5
obj2 sharedVariable: 20
```

As you can see, each instance has its own instance variable value, but all instances and the class share the class variable value.

supported_facts_labels: ['In Pharo , the terms " instance variable names " and " class variable names " refer to different types of variables with distinct properties and access scopes .', 'Class variables , on the other hand , are shared amongst all the instances of a class and the class itself .', 'Each instance has its own set of instance variables , and they can only be accessed by the instance itself or by the methods defined in its subclasses .', 'A subclass will inherit the class variables , but each subclass will have its own private copies of those variables .', 'Instance variables are private to the individual instance of a class .', 'Instance variables can not be directly accessed by other instances of the same class or by the class itself .', 'This is in contrast to Java and C++ where instance variables can be accessed by any other instance of the same class .', 'Class variables are used to store data that needs to be shared amongst all instances and the class .']

title: Why do methods return "self" by default in Smalltalk?
body:
Background

In Smalltalk, if you don't excplicitly return anything then the message passing evaluates to the receiver (or "self" in the message context).

For example, given this method:

MyClass >> myMethod
  Transcript show: 'hello'; cr.

Evaluating (doint "print-it") this:

| myInstance |
myInstance := MyClass new.
myInstance myMethod.

If <print-it> is done to the last invocation, then the result would be the instance itself.

Questions

  • Why was this designed this way?
  • What is the idea behind it?
  • What was the philosophical background?
  • What are the practical benefits from it? Is it to facilitate method chaining?
stackoverflow_answer:
One very simple reason has not been stated yet: In the Virtual Machine, returning self is simpler and more efficient than returning any other object.

Smalltalk byte codes implement a stack machine. That means arguments are passed by pushing them onto a stack, rather than putting them into registers. In addition to the arguments as listed in the method signature, a hidden argument is always passed, which is the receiver of the message. So even for unary methods (those without arguments) the receiver is pushed onto the stack, then the method is executed, and the receiver value on the stack is how the method knows "self". By returning "self" if no explicit return statement is given, the VM can just leave the "self" oop on the stack, which saves at least one memory store operation. So from an efficiency and simplicity standpoint, returning "self" is the most elegant thing to do.
explanation:
In Smalltalk, methods return the receiver (represented by the "self" pseudo-variable) by default if no explicit return statement is defined. This design choice encourages objects to have small methods and delegate tasks to other objects, rather than implementing huge, procedural methods (Pelrine, 100-103).

The reason for this is that each object may have its unique implementation for responding to a specific message. For instance, the SmallInteger 3 and the Point (1@2) have their individual methods for handling the message '+ 4'. Therefore, Pharo's message-sending model encourages a style where objects tend to have small methods (Pelrine, 101).

Moreover, this design choice ensures that when a class reimplements a superclass method, the receiver is still returned, as in the case of WebServer and its subclass ReliableWebServer. Using 'self' guarantees that the correct object is returned as the receiver (pp. 95-96).

An example of this can be seen in the Morph class hierarchy, where both Morph and EllipseMorph implement the defaultColor method. The method defined in EllipseMorph overrides the one in Morph, resulting in an ellipse having a yellow default color instead of the blue one for a morph (p. 104).

Additionally, this design choice allows for method chaining, as the result of a method call can be directly used as the receiver for the next method call. For instance, given a MyClass instance 'myInstance', 'myInstance myMethod1' can be followed by 'myInstance myMethod2', making the code more concise and readable.

In summary, methods in Smalltalk (Pharo) return the receiver by default to encourage objects to have small methods, delegate tasks, and follow the message-sending model (Pelrine, 101). This design choice provides a uniform and simple programming model in which everything is an object and objects communicate only by sending each other messages.

title: How do we check in pharo if an element already exist in a set
body:
Say I have a function that continually add a random number to a collection after each refresh.

Myfunction
|list length data |

z := Set new.
x := 8.
data:= received data after each refresh.
length := data size.
1 to: length do:[:k | list := data at: k.
    list < x
    "How to check if this data already exist and not add"
        ifTrue:[z add: list]

How can I check in Pharo if a number already exists and if so it should not add it again?
stackoverflow_answer:
Sets and other basic collections are part of common Smalltalk. You should have a look at the Smalltalk documentation (e.g., the Smalltalk bluebook), particularly the references for each of the data types you are attempting to use as well as other related ones.

If you read the documentation for Set you'll find a message #includes: which will return true if the argument is in the set, and return false if it isn't. That would look like:

(z includes: list) ifTrue: [ z add: list ]

But, a Set by definition contains unique elements. Therefore, if you attempt to add an element that's already in the set, Smalltalk will not add it again and not give you an error if you attempt to do so. So you don't need to check to see if the set already includes list. You can just add it.

Then you are doing your data traversal the long, hard way. You don't need to get the length then loop on an index from 1 to that length. I assume your data is some kind of Smalltalk Collection, so you can just use the #do: message. The #do: message allows you to iterate a block over each element of the collection:

data do: [ :each | each < x ifTrue: [ z add: each ] ]

This replaces everything you have shown starting at length := ....
explanation:
To check if an element already exists in a set in Pharo, you can utilize the set operations provided by Pharo's Collection messages, such as `includes:`. The `includes:` message tests if an element is present in the set and returns a Boolean value.

For example, if you have a set named `z` and you want to check if a number `x` already exists in it, you can use the following code snippet:

```smalltalk
z add: x ifFalse: [ z add: x ].
z includes: x
```

This code first attempts to add the number `x` to the set `z`. If the number is already present in the set, the `add:` message will not do anything. The `includes:` message is then used to check if the number exists in the set.

Alternatively, if you want to initialize the set with some predefined values and test for membership, you can create a Set using the `newFrom:` class message or the `asSet:` conversion message, as demonstrated in the text:

```smalltalk
|list length data s|
s := Set newFrom: #(1 2 3 1 4).
s includes: 4
>>> true
```

In this example, the set `s` is created using the `newFrom:` message and initialized with the elements #(1 2 3 1 4). The `includes:` message is then used to check if the number 4 is present in the set.

Note that the set `s` is a mathematical set that does not allow duplicate elements and has no order, while a Bag is a collection that does allow duplicates. The set operations `union:`, `intersection:`, and `includes:` are implemented for all kinds of collections, including Sets and Bags.

title: How to implement a switch in Pharo Smalltalk
body:
I'm trying to parse a command and an int to make a "turtle" move on a board. I'm a bit overwhelmed, since it's not throwing an exception, and I can't even figure out how to open the debugger without one.

My code:

"only should begin parsing on new line"
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    "splits commands based on new lines"
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        "should split into command and value for command"
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        "command"
        bo := ci at: 1.
        val := ci at: 2.
        "value for command"
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ bo = 'down' "attempted switch"
            ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ]
            ifFalse: [
              bo = 'left'
                  ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ]
                  ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'right'
                        ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ]
                        ifFalse: [
                          bo = 'up'
                              ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ]
                              ifFalse: [ self assert: false ] ] ] ] ].
stackoverflow_answer:
You can do it the way you did it, and you can open a debugger by inserting a self halt statement into your code.

Usually, ifs, and moreover case-sytle ifs, are a bad sign. So what you can do is break the functionality into classes like DownMove, LeftMove and so on, and then each class will implement its own functionality when you call, for example, an execute: method that will do exactly what is needed by the command. But in your case, the approach would be cumbersome; moreover, you have very trivial actions.

You can use a dictionary with definitions. So imagine that you define an instance variable or class variable:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> [ :dist | turtle down: dist ] .
  'left' -> [ :dist | turtle left: dist ] .
  ... } asDictionary

Then in your code, you do: (moveActions at: bo) value: valInt. This snippet will give you a block (value) for a string (key), then you evaluate the block with your integer.

On the other hand, as the action pattern is the same and only message changes, you can map only the message names in the dictionary:

moveActions := {
  'down' -> #down: .
  'left' -> #left: .
  ... } asDictionary

Then you can ask your turtle to perform a message dynamically given by string: 

`turtle perform: (moveActions at: bo) with: valInt`

Also, if you want to rely on the similarity between commands that you read and messages that you send to the turtle, you can dynamically compose the message string:

`turtle perform: (bo, ':') asSymbol with: valInt`

Please note that this is not recommended in your case, as, first of all, you are coupling user input and your code, i.e. if you decide to change the user command from down to moveDown, you will have to change your method name from down: to moveDown:. Also, this approach allows the user to "inject" bad code into your system, as he can write a command like become 42, which will result in the code:

`turtle perform: #become: with: 42`

which will swap pointers between the turtle object and 42. Or you can think about even worse cases. But I hope that this meta excursion was good for you. :)
explanation:
To implement a switch-like behavior in Pharo Smalltalk without using exceptions, you can create a subclass of SimpleSwitchMorph, override mouseMove: to do nothing, and add the desired behaviors as methods. Here's a step-by-step process based on the provided text chunks:

1. Create a new class named LOCell by modifying the class creation template as follows:
   a. Replace Object with SimpleSwitchMorph.
   b. Replace NameOfSubClass with LOCell.
   c. Add mouseAction to the list of instance variables.

2. Override the mouseMove: method in the LOCell class to do nothing:
   a. In the LOCell class definition, add the following method:
      mouseMove: ^ super mouseMove:

3. Add methods to the LOCell class for the desired behaviors (e.g., move the turtle left, right, up, or down):
   a. Modify the class creation template to add methods for the desired behaviors.
   b. Use the message template to create a new method for each behavior, replacing "doSomething:" with the name of the behavior.

4. Use the methods you've created in your code to parse the command and the int:
   a. Replace the conditional statements in your code with messages to the appropriate methods in the LOCell class.

5. Test your code by running it in the Pharo environment.

By following these steps, you'll be able to implement a switch-like behavior in Pharo Smalltalk without relying on exceptions or opening the debugger.

Here's an example of what the code could look like:

```smalltalk
| aTurtleProgram commands bo val valInt turtle |
endsWithNewLine:= aTurtleProgram endsWith: String cr.
endsWithNewLine ifTrue:[
    commands := aTurtleProgram splitOn: String cr.
    commands do:[:com |
        i := com splitOn: ' '.
        bo := i at: 1.
        val := i at: 2.
        valInt := val asInteger.
        ^ (bo = 'down' ifTrue: [ turtle down: valInt ] ifFalse: [
            bo = 'left' ifTrue: [ turtle left: valInt ] ifFalse: [
                bo = 'right' ifTrue: [ turtle right: valInt ] ifFalse: [
                    bo = 'up' ifTrue: [ turtle up: valInt ] ] ] ] ]

Object subclass: #LOCell
instanceVariableNames: 'mouseAction'
classVariableNames: ''
category: 'PBE-LightsOut'

LOCell class >> new: ^ self new initialize.

LOCell >> initialize:
    super initialize.
    mouseAction := [ : | self doMouseAction ].

LOCell >> doMouseAction:
    ^ self

LOCell >> left: aNumber
    ^ ...

LOCell >> right: aNumber
    ^ ...

LOCell >> up: aNumber
    ^ ...

LOCell >> down: aNumber
    ^ ...
```

Replace the placeholders `...` with the implementation of the respective methods.

Replace NameOfSubClass with LOCell .', 'In the LOCell class definition , add the following method :         mouseMove : ^ super mouseMove :    3 .']

title: In Pharo/Smalltalk: How to read a file with a specific encoding?
body:
I am currently reading a file like this:

dir := FileSystem disk workingDirectory.
stream := (dir / 'test.txt' ) readStream.
line := stream nextLine.

This works when the file is utf-8 encoded but I could not find out what to do when the file has another encoding.
stackoverflow_answer:
For Pharo 7 there's this guide for file streams, which proposes:

('test.txt' asFileReference)
    readStreamEncoded: 'cp-1250' do: [ :stream |
        stream upToEnd ].
explanation:
To read a file with a specific encoding in Pharo/Smalltalk, you can use the `FileStream class` and send the message `converter:` followed by an instance of the desired encoding converter. Here's an example using the `Latin1TextConverter`:

```smalltalk
| stream |
stream := FileStream readOnlyFileNamed: 'test.txt'.
stream converter: Latin1TextConverter new.
contents := stream contents.
stream close.
```

In the provided text, the example on page 233 shows how to write a file with a specific encoding, but it doesn't cover reading with a specific encoding explicitly. The text does mention that FileStreams are character-based by default, so you can use the converter messages to handle other encodings when reading or writing.

If you want to write a file with a specific encoding, you can use the `FileStream forceNewFileNamed:` message as shown below:

```smalltalk
| stream |
stream := FileStream forceNewFileNamed: 'test.txt'.
stream converter: UTF8TextConverter new. % or any other encoding converter
stream nextPutAll: 'Your text here'.
stream close.
```

Keep in mind that you'll need to import the corresponding TextConverter classes for the specific encoding you want to use. You can find the TextConverter classes in the Pharo Image under the `Collections` > `Text` > `TextConverters` category.

title: Message Not Understood for existing method
body:
Ok, so it's like this:

receiver selectors

returns

#(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name)

But 

receiver expression

throws MessageNotUnderstood.

Any ides how to fix this and why this can be caused?
stackoverflow_answer:
You are probably sending the message expression to a class instead of an instance of that class.

If the result of evaluating receiver selectors is #(#expression #expression: #mondrianLabel #accept: #name) then receiver is most probably a class. The message #selectors is implemented on the class Behavior and returns the selectors of the instance methods of the class. In other words: instances of the class (or any of its subclasses) understand the messages listed.

Hence, if you evaluate receiver expression, then you will get a message not understood exception because the message #expression is defined on the instance of the class that is the value of your receiver variable.
explanation:
The question pertains to receiving a "Message Not Understood" error for an existing method in Pharo Smalltalk. This error occurs when the method being searched for is not found in the object or any of its superclasses, and the default behavior is to trigger the debugger. However, developers can intercept and handle this error by overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in their subclasses.

According to the provided text from the "Pharo By Example" textbook (pp. 107-108, 312-313), when a message is not understood by an object, it will start looking for the method `doesNotUnderstand:` in its class hierarchy. If it is found, the debugger will be launched. However, if this method is overridden in any subclass of Object, the object can intercept the error and take alternative actions like forwarding the message to the original target, sending errors, or loading missing methods dynamically.

Therefore, to fix the issue, you can override the `doesNotUnderstand:` method in the receiver class and provide an alternative action to handle the error instead of triggering the debugger. For example, you could provide a default implementation that returns an error message or sends a different message to the receiver.

Here's a simple example of overriding the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to print a message instead of triggering the debugger:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: 'someVariable'
    classVariableNames: ''
    category: 'My Category'

MyClass class >> new: ^ self new initialize.

MyClass class >> initialize
    super class >> initialize.
    self class >> doesNotUnderstand: aMessageSelector
        ^ 'Unknown message: ', aMessageSelector printString.

MyClass new someVariable: 5.
MyClass new doesNotUnderstand: #nonExistentMethod.
```

In this example, we create a new class `MyClass` that overrides the `doesNotUnderstand:` method to print a message instead of triggering the debugger. When we send the non-existent message `#nonExistentMethod` to an instance of this class, it will print the message "Unknown message: nonExistentMethod" instead of triggering the debugger.

Keep in mind that this technique should be used carefully, as it can make it harder to find and debug errors in your code. It's generally recommended to provide clear error messages and let the debugger handle unexpected errors in most cases.

title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
To invoke shell commands from Squeak or Pharo, you can launch Pharo from the command line and execute shell commands within Pharo using Smalltalk code. Pharo tries to "do the right thing" when launching, but if you have multiple VMs installed, it may not be able to guess the correct one. In such cases, it is recommended to specify the image explicitly by dragging and dropping the image file onto the VM or specifying the image on the command line.

For Linux and Mac OS X systems, you can launch Pharo from the command line by providing the path to the Pharo executable and the path to the Pharo image file. For example, on Linux:
```bash
./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image
```
and on Mac OS X:
```bash
Pharo5.0.app/Contents/MacOS/Pharo Pharo5.0.image
```
Once Pharo is launched, you can execute shell commands using Smalltalk code. One way to do this is by creating a Process class and using its `openReadWrite` message to execute shell commands and capture their output. Here's an example:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #Process
    instanceVariableNames: 'command output error'
    classVariableNames: ''
    poolDictionaries: ''
    category: 'System'

Process class >> new: aString
    ^ self new initialize: [ :args | self new initialize: [ :initializer |
        initializer command: aString ] ]

Process class >> current
    ^ SystemImage current process.

Process class >> new: aString returnString: aBlock
    ^ self new: aString do: [ :process |
        | result |
        process forkAt: [ :forkedProcess |
            forkedProcess standardInput: [ :input | input nextPutAll: aString asString, String cr ];
            forkedProcess standardOutput: [ :output | output on: [ :out | out nextPutAll: aBlock value asString ]; close ];
            forkedProcess standardError: [ :error | error on: [ :err | err nextPutAll: Transcript show: 'Error: '; nextPutAll: err contents asString ]; close ];
            forkedProcess wait ]

Process class >> new: aString andWait: aBlock
    ^ self new: aString do: [ :process |
        process forkAt: [ :forkedProcess |
            forkedProcess standardInput: [ :input | input nextPutAll: aString asString, String cr ];
            forkedProcess standardOutput: [ :output | output on: [ :out | aBlock value: out ] ];
            forkedProcess standardError: [ :error | error on: [ :err | error nextPutAll: Transcript show: 'Error: '; nextPutAll: err contents asString ]; close ];
            forkedProcess wait ]

Process class >> new: aString andShowOutput: aBlock
    ^ self new: aString returnString: [ :output | aBlock value: output contents ] andWait: [ :process | process wait ]

Process class >> new: aString andShowError: aBlock
    ^ self new: aString returnString: [ :error | aBlock value: error contents ] andWait: [ :process | process wait ]

Process class >> new: aString andShowBoth: aBlock1 aBlock2
    ^ self new: aString returnString: [ :output | aBlock1 value: output contents ] andReturnString: [ :error | aBlock2 value: error contents ] andWait: [ :process | process wait ]

Process class >> currentProcess
    ^ Process new: 'ps aux | grep pharo' andShowBoth: [ :output | output lines select: [ :line | line startsWith: 'pharo' ] ] [ :error | error contents ]

Process >> initialize: spec
    super initialize.
    self command: (spec at: 2).

Process >> initialize: aBlock
    super initialize.
    self command: aBlock value asString.

Process >> forkAt: aBlock
    | forkProcess |
    forkProcess := Process new: self command returnString: [ :output | aBlock value: output ] andWait: [ :process | process ]
   .
    forkProcess id ifNil: [
        Transcript show: 'Forking process failed: ', self command; cr;
        ^ self ].
    ^ forkProcess.

Process >> forkAt: aBlock andWait: aBlock1
    | forkProcess |
    forkProcess := Process new: self command returnString: aBlock andWait: aBlock1 andWait: [ :process | process ]
   .
    forkProcess id ifNil: [
        Transcript show: 'Forking process failed: ', self command; cr;
        ^ self ].
    ^ forkProcess.

Process >> forkAt: aBlock andShowOutput: aBlock1
    | forkProcess |
    forkProcess := Process new: self command returnString: [ :output | aBlock1 value: output ] andWait: aBlock andWait: [ :process | process ]
   .
    forkProcess id ifNil: [
        Transcript show: 'Forking process failed: ', self command; cr;
        ^ self ].
    ^ forkProcess.

Process >> forkAt: aBlock andShowError: aBlock1
    | forkProcess |
    forkProcess := Process new: self command returnString: [ :error | aBlock1 value: error ] andWait: aBlock andWait: [ :process | process ]
   .
    forkProcess id ifNil: [
        Transcript show: 'Forking process failed: ', self command; cr;
        ^ self ].
    ^ forkProcess.

Process >> forkAt: aBlock andShowBoth: aBlock1 aBlock2
    | forkProcess |
    forkProcess := Process new: self command returnString: [ :output | aBlock1 value: output ] andReturnString: [ :error | aBlock2 value: error ] andWait: aBlock andWait: [ :process | process ]
   .
    forkProcess id ifNil: [
        Transcript show: 'Forking process failed: ', self command; cr;
        ^ self ].
    ^ forkProcess.

Process >> command
    ^ command.

Process >> output
    ^ output.

Process >> error
    ^ error.
```

With the above code, you can execute shell commands and capture their output or error messages using various methods provided by the `Process` class. For example, to execute `ls -l` and print its output to the Transcript, you can do:

```smalltalk
Process new: 'ls -l' andShowOutput: [ :output | Transcript show: output ]
```

To execute a command and capture both the output and error messages, you can do:

```smalltalk
Process new: 'ls -l' andShowBoth: [ :output | Transcript show: 'Output:'; cr; Transcript show: output ] [ :error | Transcript show: 'Error:'; cr; Transcript show: error ]
```

- Page 13: "Sending Messages"
- Page 14: "Pharo Launcher"

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
To create a new Date or DateAndTime object with specified day, month, and year values in Pharo / Squeak, there isn't a predefined method that takes three integers directly. However, you can achieve this by using a combination of string manipulation and date format recognizers.

First, you need to create a date format recognizer using regular expressions. The recognizer below matches the date string in the format of 'Month Day, Year':

```smalltalk
| date result |
date := '[Jan|Feb|Mar|Apr|May|Jun|Jul|Aug|Sep|Oct|Nov|Dec]\s+(\d\d?)\s*,\s*19(\d\d)' asRegex.
```

Next, you can use the `matches:` message to find the date in a given string:

```smalltalk
| dateString result |
dateString := 'Aug 6, 1996'.
result := (date matches: dateString) ifTrue: [ date subexpression: 4. (date subexpression: 2). (date subexpression: 3) ] ifFalse: ['no match'].
```

The `result` will be a three-element array containing the year, month, and day.

Alternatively, you can use the inspector to change the values of an existing Date or DateAndTime object. By typing `DateAndTime now` in a playground, then right-clicking and choosing "Inspect it" or "Do it and go," you'll be able to modify its instance variables directly using the inspector window. Make sure you have accessor methods defined for those variables.

Keep in mind that modifying system objects should be done with care and only when necessary. It's essential to save your image before experimenting with the inspector.

Here is an example of how to test the date recognizer:

```smalltalk
| dateString result |
dateString := 'Aug 6, 1996'.
result := (date matches: dateString).
result ifNotNil: [ :match | Transcript show: 'Year: ', match subexpression: 4; Transcript cr. Transcript show: 'Month: ', match subexpression: 2; Transcript cr. Transcript show: 'Day: ', match subexpression: 3; Transcript cr ].
```

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
To sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk, you cannot directly get an OrderedCollection of the keys with the values in descending order using a single message. However, you can achieve this by converting the Dictionary to an Array of associations first, then creating an OrderedCollection from that.

First, you need to create an Array of associations from the Dictionary using the message `asArray`. Then, send the `asSortedCollection` message to the Array to sort it based on the values in descending order. Lastly, you can extract the keys from the sorted OrderedCollection to get the desired OrderedCollection of keys in descending order based on their corresponding values.

Here is an example:

```smalltalk
| aDictionary sortedOrderedCollection |
aDictionary := Dictionary newFrom: { #blue -> Color blue. #yellow -> Color yellow }.
| sortedAssociations sortedOrderedCollection |
sortedAssociations := aDictionary asArray.
sortedOrderedCollection := sortedAssociations asSortedCollection: [ :assoc1 :assoc2 | assoc1 value asNumber > assoc2 value asNumber ].
| keys |
keys := OrderedCollection new.
sortedOrderedCollection do: [ :assoc | keys add: assoc key ].
keys
```

The `sortedOrderedCollection` will contain the `OrderedCollection` of associations (key-value pairs) sorted based on their values in descending order. The last step extracts the keys from this collection and stores them in a new `OrderedCollection` named `keys`.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can follow these steps:

1. Ensure that both arrays have the same number of elements.
2. Iterate through each element of the first array and check if it exists in the second array. If it does, continue iterating. If not, then the arrays do not contain the same elements.

Here is an example implementation in Pharo Smalltalk:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).

arr1 size = arr2 size ifFalse: [ ^ false ].

arr1 do: [ :a | (arr2 includes: a) ifFalse: [ ^ false ]. ].

^ true
```

The `includes:` message is used to check if an array contains a specific element.

However, it is essential to note that both arrays must have the same objects as elements, not just objects with the same values. If the arrays contain different objects with equal values, they will not be considered equal even if they contain the same elements. In that case, you will need to override the `hash` method for the objects to make them comparable in sets or dictionaries.

For more information, you can refer to the Pharo By Example textbook on pages 181-183, 200, and 218-219.

title: Squeak or Pharo for the beginning Smalltalker?
body:
I am a Rubyist that is just starting to dabble in Smalltalk and am trying to chose an implementation to experiment with. It seems like Squeak or Pharo would be a good choice, but I'm having trouble finding current information to help decide which one would be better. Much of what I find via Google searches is from a couple of years ago and may no longer be relevant.

Is there a clear favorite these days? If not, what are the pros and cons of each?
stackoverflow_answer:
To certain extent, it is a matter of personal taste. Pharo is fork of Squeak intended make it more modern version of Smalltalk, sometimes at expense of not sticking to backward compatibility to Squeak.

Pharo being a fork, also means that they share much in common, from VM to huge parts of class library. Many add on packages work with both of them, and some are developed with Squeak as primary dev platform and I guess a slightly larger number of them are developed in Pharo (Seaside, Pier, etc). Both variants have free intro books: Squeak by Example, and Pharo by Example, but Pharo one seems more up to date.

Personally I prefer Pharo since it gives me cleaner and more modern feeling.
explanation: Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed programming language inspired by Smalltalk. It is highly portable and can run on various operating systems including OS X, Windows, Linux, Android, iOS, and Raspberry Pi. Pharo is the reference implementation for the Seaside web development framework. Pharo's ecosystem consists of six fundamental elements: a dynamically-typed language with a simple syntax, a live coding environment, a powerful IDE, a rich library, a culture of changes and improvements, and a welcoming community. Pharo is unique in that it offers a live coding environment, allowing programmers to modify code while the code executes without interrupting their workflow. Pharo's community values and encourages changes and improvements, and offers a stable platform for professional software development and research into dynamic languages and environments. To get started, you can download the Seaside One-Click Experience, which includes a prepackaged version of Pharo with many Seaside-related packages preloaded, as well as a helpful Seaside control panel. (References: Preface, 1.1, 2.1, 13.2)

title: Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?
body:
Smalltalk - Is it possible to add a string to a String instance via a method?

Essentially I'd like something along the lines of:

renderThisOn: aString

aString append: 'whatever text I want'

Essentially I'd like a String instance (ByteString, etc) to behave like the "html" object in Seaside. I pass it on as an argument to multiple methods, each adding some information to it.
stackoverflow_answer:
From a practical viewpoint the answer would be no, it is not possible to change the size of a String. You can modify the characters of the String though:

a := 'abc'.
a at: 2 put: $x.
a = 'axc' "true"

Therefore, when you concatenate two strings you get a third one, while the others two remain unchanged

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
u := s , t.
s = 'abc'. "true"
t = 'def'. "true"

Having said this, there is actually a way to do grow (or shrink) a String. The idea is to use become: (or becomeForward:). This message will replace all references to the receiver with references to the argument. In your case:

s := 'abc'.
t := 'def'.
assoc := s -> 3 "referene to s"
s become: s , t.
s = 'abcdef'. "true"
assoc key == s "true"

The reason why I started my answer by saying that you cannot change the string's size is because in the vast majority of cases the use of become: is overkilling and the recommended practice is to review the code and eliminate the need for modifying the structure of an object.
explanation:
In Pharo, a String is an object that represents a sequence of characters. It is a mutable and indexed collection of Characters. The String class is abstract, and Pharo strings are actually instances of the concrete classes ByteString or Symbol.

To modify a String in Pharo, you can use several methods. One common method is the `at:`:`put:` message to replace a character at a specific index. Another method is `replaceAll:`:`with:` or `replaceFrom:`:`to:`:`with:` to replace substrings. For example:

```smalltalk
| s |
s := 'No hurries'.
s replaceAll: $n with: $N.
s
"No hurries" -> "No hurries"
s replaceFrom: 4 to: 5 with: 'wo'.
s
"No worries"
```

Another method is `copyReplaceAll:`:`with:` to create a new String with some substrings replaced. It's important to note that the arguments are substrings rather than individual characters.

You can also modify a String by concatenating it with other strings using the `,` operator or the `,:` message:

```smalltalk
| s1 s2 |
s1 := 'hello'.
s2 := ' world'.
s1 , s2
"hello world"
s1 copyReplaceAll: 'world' with: 'moon'.
s1
"hello moon"
s1 , s2
"hello moon world"
s1 copyReplaceAll: 'world' with: 'moon'. , s2
"hello moon moon"
```

Strings are also comparable, so you can use methods like `<`, `>`, `=`, etc. to compare them. Additionally, you can use the `asSortedCollection:` message to sort a collection of strings or any other comparable objects.

The syntax for creating a String and a SortedCollection in Pharo is different. To create a String, you can use single quotes:

```smalltalk
| s |
s := 'hello'.
s class
"String"
```

To create a SortedCollection, you need to create an instance of the `SortedCollection` class and add elements to it:

```smalltalk
| s sc |
s := 'hello'.
sc := SortedCollection new.
sc add: s.
sc asSortedCollection
"('hello')"
```

To get a String back from a SortedCollection, you can use the `as: String` message or the `String newFrom:` message:

```smalltalk
| sc s |
sc := #('hello' 'world' 'moon') asSortedCollection.
s := sc as: String.
s
"hello"
s := String newFrom: sc.
s
"hello"
```

There are also other ways to create and manipulate collections in Pharo, such as using arrays, dictionaries, or other collection classes. The Pharo documentation provides more detailed information on these topics.

title: Check if an object is an instance of a given class or of a subclass of it
body:
Is there an easy way to do this in Smalltalk? I'm 80% sure that there is some method but can't find it anywhere.

I know that I can use

(instance class = SomeClass) ifTrue:

And I know that I can use superclass etc... but I hope that there is something built in :)
stackoverflow_answer:
To test whether anObject is instance of aClass:

(anObject isMemberOf: aClass)

To test whether it is an instance of aClass or one of it subclasses:

(anObject isKindOf: aClass)
explanation:
In Pharo, there are several methods to query the class of an object. These methods include `class`, `isMemberOf:`, `isKindOf:`, and `respondsTo:`.

`class` is a message that returns the class of an object. For instance, `1 class` returns `SmallInteger`.

`isMemberOf:` checks if an object is an instance of a specific class. For example, `1 isMemberOf: SmallInteger` returns `true`.

`isKindOf:` returns `true` if the receiver's class is either the same as or a subclass of the argument class. For instance, `1 isKindOf: Object` and `1 isKindOf: Number` both return `true`.

`respondsTo:` checks if an object understands a given message selector. For instance, `1 respondsTo: #+` returns `true`.

Additionally, there are methods to query all instances of a class, such as `Object new class allInstances` and `ByteString allInstances`, which can be useful for debugging and code discovery.

It is important to note that classes are objects too, and every object is an instance of a class. Classes define the structure and behavior of their instances via instance variables and methods. Each class has a unique metaclass that defines its structure and behavior.

Overall, there are various methods to query the class of an object in Pharo, which can be useful for development tools and debugging. However, it is important to be aware of the implications of the uniformity of the object model and the reflective nature of the system.

title: How to print an integer to transcript
body:
I've tried the code

|myNum|
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).

, but Pharo crashes upon running this code.
stackoverflow_answer:
You can't create a SmallInteger using new:. You already have the number you want to print. It's already a SmallInteger. Just use it like such:

Transcript show: 0 printString
explanation:
The Interval class in Pharo represents ranges of numbers. For example, the interval of numbers from 1 to 100 can be defined as `(1 to: 100)`. The `printOn:` method of the Interval class behaves by printing the string representation of the starting point, the string ' to: ', and the ending point of the interval. For instance, `(1 to: 10) printOn: aStream` will output the string '1 to: 10' when executed.

To print an integer to the Transcript, you can use the following code:

```smalltalk
| myNum |
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString).
```

However, the code you provided crashes Pharo. The reason for this is that you need to use `Print it` instead of just sending the `printString` message to the integer directly. To print an integer in the Transcript, use the following code:

```smalltalk
| myNum |
myNum := SmallInteger new: 0.
Transcript show: (myNum printString). "Use 'Print it' instead of just 'printString'"
Transcript cr. "Add a newline character after printing the integer"
```

Alternatively, you can use the convenience method `Transcript show:` followed by the integer directly:

```smalltalk
Transcript show: 0.
```

Integers in Pharo can be used for various purposes, including specifying iterative behavior using the `timesRepeat:` message, and performing arithmetic operations such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, and division. Additionally, Pharo has virtual machine primitives for integer arithmetic and bit manipulation.

title: Regex: Matching all words in a phrase
body:
Is this possible?

For a sentence such as hello how are you, I'd like my regular expression to return hello how are you. It only ever returns just hello and not the other words.

My Regex: 

[A-Za-z]*

Any help is greatly appreciated. Thanks! If it matters, I'm using Pharo Smalltalk. I've been testing in c# too.
stackoverflow_answer:
If you only need split the sentence by spaces this can be done using string.Split() method:

var s = "hello how are you";
var words = s.Split();

If you want to use regular expressions:

var s = "hello how are you";
var regex = "\\w+";
var words = Regex.Matches(s, regex).Cast<Match>().Select(m => m.Value);
explanation:
A regular expression (regex) is a pattern used to identify and manipulate strings that match a certain pattern. In Pharo Smalltalk, regex is supported through the Regex package. Regex is widely used in many scripting languages such as Perl, Python, and Ruby for tasks such as identifying strings that match a certain pattern, checking input format, and rewriting strings to new formats.

To match all words in a phrase, we can use regex with word boundary (\b) to separate words. For example, the regex '\w+' matches any word consisting of one or more alphanumeric characters. However, if we want to match all words in a phrase, we need to use regex to look for word boundaries (\b) instead of just matching alphanumeric characters.

The example provided in the textbook uses the regex '.*\bw.*' to match all words in a phrase. \b matches the word boundary, and . matches any character except a newline. This regex will return 'hello', 'how', and 'you' for the input 'hello how are you'.

A character set is another component of regex. A character set is a string of characters enclosed in square brackets. It matches any single character if it appears between the brackets. For example, [01] matches either 0 or 1. We can also use ranges (pairs of characters separated by a hyphen), such as [0-9], which is equivalent to [0123456789].

The textbook also mentions that regex supports various messages such as prefixMatchesRegex:, matchesRegexIgnoringCase:, matchesPrefix:, and allRegexMatches:, which can be used to access and manipulate regex matches in different ways. For example, allRegexMatches: returns a collection of all matches of a regex within a receiver string.

Overall, regex is a powerful tool in Pharo for manipulating and validating strings based on patterns. By using characters and character sets, as well as various regex messages, we can identify and work with specific strings in our code.

title: What is the difference between self and yourself in Smalltalk?
body:
In Smalltalk, there are two terms often found within a method body: self and yourself.

What is the difference between them?
stackoverflow_answer:
The reserved word self is a pseudo variable (you cannot assign to it) that refers to the current receiver of the method where it is used. On the other side yourself is a message you can send to any object to get that very same object.

The implementation of yourself is

yourself
    ^self

meaning that the message yourself will behave as I just explained.

The reason why yourself exists is to support message cascading, where you put it as the last message to make sure the resulting expression will answer with the receiver:

^receiver
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself

If msg2 might answer with something different from the receiver you can append the yourself message to ignore that answer and return receiver instead. Of course you could have achieved the same result by writing:

receiver
   msg1;
   msg2.
^receiver

Because of the simplicity of these two examples, it might be hard to understand what the advantage would be. However, consider that receiver is not a variable but a complex expression, something like.

^(self msg: arg1 arg: arg2)
   msg1;
   msg2;
   yourself.

Without using yourself you would have to add a temporary to save the value of the receiver to achieve the same:

| answer |
answer := self msg: arg1 arg: arg2.
answer
   msg1;
   msg2.
^answer

which is a little bit more verbose.

To summarize, self is a reserved word that refers to the current receiver and yourself is just a regular method that is there just for convenience.
explanation:
Self and super are both keywords in Pharo, representing the receiver of the message in different contexts. Self always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver. Super, on the other hand, also refers to the receiver of the current method but when you send a message to super, the method-lookup changes so that it starts from the superclass of the class containing the method that uses super.

For instance, in the context of a message send, if a method in a subclass does not find the method it is looking for, it will then check its superclass for the method. This is called a super send. In contrast, the meaning of self is dynamic and always represents the receiver of the currently executing message, so all messages sent to self are looked up by starting in the receiver's class.

Here's an example to illustrate this: Suppose we have a `Shape` class with two subclasses `Circle` and `Ellipse`. The `Shape` class has a method `foo` and the `Circle` class overrides it. If an `Ellipse` instance sends the message `foo`, the method lookup will start in the `Ellipse` class, since that's the receiver. If the method is not found there, it will then look in the superclass `Circle`, and if still not found, it will go up the inheritance hierarchy to the `Shape` class. This is the concept of super sends and how it differs from self.

In summary, self always refers to the receiver of the currently executing method and the method lookup starts in the class of the receiver. Super, on the other hand, represents the receiver of the message and the method lookup starts in the superclass of the class of the method where the super send occurs.

title: Smalltalk initialization variables
body:
In languages like Java and C++ we give parameters to constructors.

How do you do this in Pharo Smalltalk?

I want something like 

|aColor|
aColor = Color new 'red'.

Or is this bad practice and should I always do

|aColor|
aColor = Color new.
aColor name:= red.d
stackoverflow_answer:
The short answer is that you can do pretty much the same in Smalltalk. From the calling code it would look like:

aColor := Color named: 'Red'.

The long answer is that in Smalltalk you don't have constructors, at least not in the sense that you have a special message named after the class. What you do in Smalltalk is defining class-side messages (i.e. messages understood by the class, not the instance[*]) where you can instantiate and configure your instances. Assuming that your Color class has a name instance variable and a setter for it, the #named: method would be implemented like:

(class) Color>>named: aName
| color |
color := self new.
color name: aName.
^color.

Some things to note:

  • We are using the #new message sent to the class to create a new instance. You can think of the #new message as the primitive way for creating objects (hint: you can browse the implementors of the #new message to see how it is implemented).
  • We can define as many class methods as we want to create new 'configured' instances (e.g. Color fromHexa:) or return pre-created ones (e.g. Color blue).
  • You can still create an uninitialized instance by doing Color new. If you want to forbid that behavior then you must override the #new class message.

There are many good books that you can read about Smalltalk basics at Stef's Free Online Smalltalk Books

[*] This is quite natural due to the orthogonal nature on Smalltalk, since everything (including classes) is an object. If you are interested check Chapter 13 of Pharo by Example or any other reference to classes and metaclasses in Smalltalk.

HTH
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, you can initialize instance variables using several ways, but there is no direct equivalent to giving parameters to constructors like in languages such as Java or C++. Instead, you can use instance variable declarations in the class definition, or initialize them in the class side of the class.

For example, you can define an instance variable `aColor` of type `Color` and initialize it to the color 'red' using the following syntax:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: 'aColor'
    classVariableNames: ''
    package: 'MyPackage'.

MyClass class >> new: aValue
    ^ self new initialize: aValue.

MyClass class >> initialize
    self initializeColorRegistry.
    aColor := Color new: 'red'.

MyClass new
>>> an instance of MyClass with the color 'red' initialized
```

Alternatively, you can initialize the `aColor` instance variable in the class side method `initialize` and then call it explicitly:

```smalltalk
Object subclass: #MyClass
    instanceVariableNames: 'aColor'
    classVariableNames: ''
    package: 'MyPackage'.

MyClass class >> new: aValue
    ^ self new initialize.

MyClass class >> initialize
    self initializeColorRegistry.
    aColor := Color new.

MyClass new initialize.
>>> an instance of MyClass with the color 'nil' initialized, then set to 'red'

MyClass new initialize: 'red'.
>>> an instance of MyClass with the color 'red' initialized
```

However, it is generally recommended to avoid initializing instance variables directly and instead provide accessor methods or use class variables for shared data.

Regarding your question about defining a global variable, you can define a global by simply assigning a value to an identifier that has not been previously declared. The Pharo parser will then offer to declare the global for you. For example:

```smalltalk
| aGlobalVariable |
aGlobalVariable := 'Hello, World!'.
Smalltalk globals at: #MyGlobal put: aGlobalVariable.

Transcript show: MyGlobal.
>>> 'Hello, World!' displayed in the Transcript
```

You can also remove a global variable using the same method:

```smalltalk
Smalltalk globals removeKey: #MyGlobal.
```

However, it is generally recommended to limit the use of global variables and instead use class variables or singletons for shared data.
