title: Invoking shell commands from Squeak or Pharo
body: How can you invoke shell commands from Squeak and Pharo? Do these environments have anything in them like the system() function in certain unix languages to run external shell commands, or the backticks (can't make them here do to the editor, but what you get when you push the key left of "1" and above "TAB") to capture the output of commands?
stackoverflow_answer: In Squeak you can use CommandShell, but I don't know what (if anything) is available for Pharo at this time.
explanation:
Pharo is a modern, open-source, dynamically typed programming language that supports live coding, inspired by Smalltalk. Pharo is composed of six fundamental elements: a dynamically-typed language, a live coding environment, a powerful IDE, a rich library, a culture, and a community. The Pharo environment includes tools such as the System Browser, Finder, inspector, and debugger.

To launch Pharo from Linux, you can use the command line by specifying the path to the Pharo image file. For example, `./pharo shared/Pharo5.0.image`. Alternatively, you can double-click the Pharo executable script or icon if it is in your default PATH.

Pharo offers three ways to interact with the system using a mouse or other pointing device: clicking, dragging, and right-clicking. Clicking is the most commonly used interaction, which is equivalent to left-clicking on a single mouse button without any modifier keys. For example, clicking on the background of the Pharo window brings up the World menu.

The World menu contains several core tools in Pharo, including the System Browser, Playground, Monticello package manager, and others. We will discuss these tools in more detail in the coming chapters. To use the Playground tool, you can open it from the World menu and use it to write and evaluate snippets of code. For instance, you can trigger the Pharo tutorial by entering and evaluating the code `PharoTutorial new open`.

Additionally, Pharo has a rich library that provides access to the FFI (Foreign Function Interface), which enables calling external code written in other programming languages. This makes Pharo a powerful tool for software development and research into dynamic languages and environments.

title: How to set day month and year values for a Date or DataAndTime object in Smalltalk (Pharo / Squeak)
body:
I'm trying to find a a pre-defined method for objects of Class Date or Class DateAndTime, that allows me to create an new Date (or a new DateAndTime) by supplying three integers: one integer for the day of the month (1-31); one for the month (1 - 12); and a four-digit integer for the year.

( The closest I've found so far is Integer>>asYear )

Is there a method that can set all three parameters at once?
stackoverflow_answer:
If I understand you correctly, you are trying to create an instance of Date from three integers representing the day, month and year of said date.

When facing a question like this you can browse the class, Date in this case, and check its protocol for instance creation methods. In Pharo there are several methods in this category, but it is easy so see (I think) that #year:month:day looks like a good candidate. So, you can try it. Just evaluate the expression

Date year: 2015 month: 12 day: 31

and see what happens (you can inspect or print it to see the result).

You will also find #newDay:month:year as another good candidate. You could try it too. Or you could just see that it sends our previous message and thus it is just a synonymous (which is present for compatibility with other dialects that support the Smalltalk-80/ANSI specification).
explanation:
In Pharo, you can create instances of Date or DateAndTime objects by using the new: message with the number of seconds since the Unix Epoch (January 1, 1970) as an argument. For example:

`| date | date := Date new: 1632048400.`

You can also create a Date object using the year, month, and day messages:

`| date | date := Date new: year new: month new: day.`

To create a DateAndTime object, you can use the new: message with the number of milliseconds since the Unix Epoch:

`| datetime | datetime := DateAndTime new: 1632048400000.`

Or, you can create a DateAndTime object using the year, month, day, hour, minute, and second messages:

`| datetime | datetime := DateAndTime new: year new: month new: day at: hour at: minute second: second.`

Regarding your question about accessing a global object in Smalltalk, the example given accesses the Boolean global by sending the at: message to the Smalltalk globals dictionary using the symbol #Boolean as the key.

`| boolean | boolean := Smalltalk globals at: #Boolean.`

Globals in Smalltalk can be defined by performing an assignment to a capitalized but undeclared identifier. Pharo will offer to declare the global for you. If you want to define a global programmatically, you can use the Smalltalk globals dictionary to put and remove key-value pairs.

`| Smalltalk | Smalltalk globals at: #AGlobalName put: nil.`

`Smalltalk globals removeKey: #AGlobalName.`

It is recommended practice to limit the use of global variables and instead use class instance variables or class variables, and provide class methods to access them.

title: How to sort a Dictionary by values in Smalltalk?
body:
I've got a Dictionary like this:

a PluggableDictionary(
    Rankable1->8.5
    Rankable2->9.0
)

I need just an OrderedCollection with the Rankable objects in descending order:

a OrderedCollection(
    Rankable2
    Rankable1
)

I noticed it is easy to sort by keys, but I found it a bit more difficult to sort by values. What is the smalltalk way of doing this?
stackoverflow_answer:
If you need one shot sorted collection in noncritical loop you might use something like this (uses pharo syntax to initialize example dictionary):

pd := PluggableDictionary newFromPairs: { 'a' . 2 . 'b' . 1 . 'c' . 3} . 

(pd associations asSortedCollection: [:x :y | x value < y value]) 
            collect: [:assoc | assoc key].

If you would need it more often, than you might consider introducing your own class that will keep this collection calculated.
explanation:
In Pharo Smalltalk, an "OrderedCollection withAll:" is a message sent to the OrderedCollection class to create a new OrderedCollection instance with the given collection of elements added in the order they appear in the original collection. Here's an example:

```smalltalk
| existingCollection newOrderedCollection |
existingCollection := #(4 5 6 1 2 3).
newOrderedCollection := OrderedCollection new: existingCollection size.
newOrderedCollection addAll: existingCollection.
newOrderedCollection
```

The result will be an OrderedCollection containing the same elements as the original collection, but in the order they were present in the original collection.

Another way to create an OrderedCollection with initial elements is by using the "withAll:" message:

```smalltalk
| newOrderedCollection |
newOrderedCollection := OrderedCollection new: 2 withAll: #(1 2).
newOrderedCollection
```

This will create a new OrderedCollection with two elements, 1 and 2.

To summarize, "OrderedCollection withAll:" is a message sent to the OrderedCollection class to create a new OrderedCollection instance with the given collection of elements added in the order they appear in the original collection. It can be used with any collection that responds to the "addAll:" message, which includes OrderedCollections, Arrays, and Sets, among others.

title: How to check if two arrays contain the same elements?
body:
Is there any simple way to check if two arrays contain the same elements?

Here is my try were I return true if arrays are not the same:

arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 2 3 4).
arr2Copy := arr2 copyFrom: 1 to: arr2 size.
arr1 size ~= arr2 size
    ifTrue: [^ true].
arr1
    do: [:a | (arr2copy removeFirst = a)
        ifFalse: [^ true]].
^false
stackoverflow_answer:
If the elements should be equal and in the same order, you can just compare the arrays with =.

If the elements should be equal and the order does not matter and there are no duplicates to be expected, use array1 asSet = arr2 asSet.

Otherwise you can check out hasEqualElements:, and asBag as well.

If the elements should be identical and in the same order, how about this?

array1 with: array2 do:
    [ :a :b | a == b ifFalse: [ ^ false ]].
 ^ true

It iterates over the two arrays simultaneously, comparing the identities of elements at the same indices. If any are not identical, return false. If no distinct elements were encountered, return true.
explanation:
To check if two arrays contain the same elements, you can follow these steps:

1. Ensure that both arrays have the same size. If not, they cannot have the same elements.
2. Iterate through both arrays using two pointers (one for each array) and compare each element.

Here's an example implementation in Pharo Smalltalk based on the provided text chunks:

```smalltalk
arr1 := #(1 3 5 6).
arr2 := #(1 3 5).

| pointer1 pointer2 |
pointer1 := arr1 first.
pointer2 := arr2 first.

[ pointer1 ~= pointer2 ifTrue: [ ^ false ].
arr1 size = arr2 size ifFalse: [ ^ false ].

[ pointer1 = arr2 at: pointer1 index ifTrue: [ pointer1 := arr1 next].
pointer2 = arr1 at: pointer2 index ifTrue: [ pointer2 := arr2 next].
pointer1 ~= nil ifTrue: [ true].
^ false ]] whileTrue.

^ true.
```

This implementation uses variables `pointer1` and `pointer2` to represent the current elements being compared from each array. It checks if both arrays have the same size and then iterates through both arrays, comparing elements at each step.

However, it is important to note that the provided text mentions that you should override the `=` method and reimplement the `hash` method for complex numbers when implementing custom equality checks. This is because of how Pharo handles object equality and identity (pp. 182-183).

If you are dealing with complex numbers or other custom objects, make sure to consider these aspects as well.
